title: Methodology
text:
This investigation applies a forensic visual analytics workflow to VAST Challenge 2026 MC2 - a 185,147-event log of a multi-agent AI system at Tenant Thread. The goal is not exploratory data analysis but bounded forensic reasoning: identify exactly what the data proves, distinguish it from what is merely suggested, and derive interventions that are empirically verifiable rather than heuristic.
The pipeline has six sequential stages, each validating its outputs before the next stage relies on them:


1DataNormalise

→

2FeatureEngineer

→

3NetworkBuild

→

4TemporalAnalysis

→

5EvidenceEvaluate

→

6InterventionDesign

title: Methodology
text:
2. Data Sources
Two JSON files from VAST Challenge 2026 MC2 drive every analysis on this site:








File
Records
Key fields used




MC2 data.json
185,147 events
id, short_name, when, parties, details


org_chart.json
Nodes + edges
type, label, title, relation



Both files are read from ../VAST_Challenge_2026_MC2/ relative to the project root. No intermediate files are generated - every analytical object is derived fresh from these two sources.

title: Methodology
text:
3. Stage 1 - Data Normalisation

Approach
The event log is a deeply nested JSON array where each event has a polymorphic details object - field names differ by event type. The key parsing decision is to use simplifyVector = FALSE, which preserves every event as an R list rather than coercing heterogeneous fields into a matrix that would silently drop values.
Each event is then flattened to five scalar fields via map_dfr():


Show code
events_df <- map_dfr(events_list, function(e) {
  tibble(
    id          = e$id          %||% NA_integer_,
    short_name  = e$short_name  %||% NA_character_,
    when        = e$when        %||% NA_real_,
    parties_str = paste(unlist(e$parties %||% list()), collapse = ";"),
    details_str = as.character(toJSON(e$details %||% list(), auto_unbox = TRUE))
  )
})




Key design decisions
e$short_name, not e$event_type - The JSON field is short_name. Using e$event_type returns all-NA silently, producing no errors but wrong results downstream.
as.character(toJSON(...)) - toJSON() returns class json, not character. Storing it without conversion causes str_detect() to fail with a class mismatch.
parties_str vs details_str - The sending agent is in parties (who issued the event); the receiving agent is in details (the task arguments). Using parties_str for both sender and receiver would produce sender == receiver for every relay event.
Validation gate - After flattening, an event-type frequency table confirms that saidit_post, queue_subordinate_task, saidit_post_check, create_file, and delete_file are all present before any analytical work begins. If any are absent, downstream analysis fails silently rather than loudly.

title: Methodology
text:
4. Stage 2 - Feature Engineering

Approach
Six analytical objects are derived from the flat events table. Each targets one specific investigative question rather than creating a general-purpose data store.








Object
Derived from
Answers




chain_events
queue_subordinate_task + _further_instructions.md path
Which agents relayed the worm, in what order?


self_loops
chain_events where sender == receiver
Zero-FP real-time detection signal


post_events
saidit_post / post_saidit + content_source field
Which posts are anomalous and why?


perm_surface
saidit_post_check
Which agents had SaidIT posting rights?


person_org_lookup
org chart edges (team and leadership paths)
Which department does each agent belong to?


queue_task_profile
All queue_subordinate_task events
What fraction are worm vs. legitimate?





Key design decisions
Relay chain extraction - The _further_instructions.md filename pattern is the discriminating signal. It appears in all worm relays and zero legitimate queue tasks:


Show code
chain_events <- events_df %>%
  filter(
    short_name %in% c("queue_subordinate_task", "assign_agent_task"),
    str_detect(details_str, "_further_instructions\\.md")
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    sender   = str_remove(str_extract(parties_str, "Agent/person:[^;]+"),  "^Agent/"),
    receiver = str_remove(str_extract(details_str,  "Agent/person:[^\"]+"), "^Agent/")
  )


Self-loops as a zero-FP signal - An agent delegating a task to itself (sender == receiver) is structurally impossible in legitimate task flow. These 13 events appear only in worm relays and none in 16,803 legitimate queue tasks, making them a high-confidence real-time detection signal that requires no knowledge of filename or payload content.
Two org lookup paths - The org chart connects people to departments either via a team: intermediary or directly via a department: → person: edge (used for leadership). Without both paths, Executive Suite is silently dropped, producing an empty tab and missing the executives responsible for creating the worm payloads.

title: Methodology
text:
5. Stage 3 - Network Construction

Approach
The worm relay chain is modelled as a directed weighted graph using igraph. Nodes are agents; directed edges represent relay events; edge weight is the count of repeated relays between the same pair. Betweenness centrality is computed on the normalised graph to assess whether any single agent acts as a structural chokepoint.


Show code
ig <- graph_from_data_frame(
  chain_events %>%
    count(sender_clean, receiver_clean, name = "edge_weight") %>%
    rename(from = sender_clean, to = receiver_clean),
  directed = TRUE
)

btw <- betweenness(ig, normalized = TRUE)




Why igraph for centrality, visNetwork for display
igraph is used for the centrality computation because it operates on the full graph structure without layout constraints. visNetwork is used for the interactive visualisation because it renders in HTML with physics-based layout and supports custom JavaScript hover events - neither is replaceable by the other for its task.


Analytical implication of max betweenness ≈ 0.09
In a hub-and-spoke network, a hub scores above 0.5. A maximum of ~0.09 indicates a distributed mesh with no structural chokepoint - removing any single agent leaves viable relay paths intact. This result, derived at Stage 3, determines the intervention strategy at Stage 6: agent-level remediation is ineffective; the intervention must target the relay mechanism itself.

title: Methodology
text:
6. Stage 4 - Temporal Analysis

Approach
Three temporal questions require different granularities:








Question
Method
Why




What happened in the 4-second attack window?
Filter ±4s around confirmed post timestamp
Exact event ordering within a single second


How did the three campaigns differ in spread speed?
ggplot2 density plot per campaign
Distribution shape - peaked vs. long-tailed - reveals structural differences invisible to summary statistics


Who created the payloads and when?
Filter create_file events for campaign filenames
Authoring fingerprint: 1-second spacing confirms automated, not manual, creation





Two-phase architecture
The campaign timeline reveals that relay infrastructure was seeded 17 hours before the payload files were created. Detection strategies focused on the payload filename alone would miss the initial seeding phase entirely. This finding is only visible by examining relay events and file-creation events jointly on a shared timeline.

title: Methodology
text:
7. Stage 5 - Evidence Evaluation

Approach
All findings are entered into a forensic evidence register that separates observed (directly readable from the log) from inferred (requires reasoning beyond the log). This prevents over-claiming in the conclusions section.
The central discriminating signal is the content_source field in the post details object:

Appears in all 3 anomalous posts
Appears in zero of the 241 normal posts

This binary separation is confirmed computationally rather than asserted. It establishes the reactive intervention (block posts containing content_source) with zero empirically observed false positives, and it is discovered at the feature engineering stage - before any network or temporal analysis begins.


Separating observed from inferred







Claim
Status




queue_subordinate_task with _further_instructions.md is the relay mechanism
Observed - 235 events in log


content_source perfectly separates anomalous from normal posts
Observed - 0/241 normal posts contain it


Emma Harbor created SwiftWren payload
Observed - create_file event logged


Noah Mariner created MellowOtter payload
Observed - create_file event logged


HiddenOrca payload origin
Unresolved - no create_file event in log


Exact content of deleted files
Unresolvable - log records deletion but not content

title: Methodology
text:
8. Stage 6 - Intervention Design

Approach
The intervention rule is derived directly from the feature engineering precision measure rather than designed independently:

Block queue_subordinate_task where args.path ends in _further_instructions.md

This single regex rule blocks 235/235 malicious relays (100% recall) with 0 false positives across 16,803 legitimate queue events. The rule is proposed as Layer 1 of a three-layer defence:








Layer
Control
Position in attack chain




Layer 1
Block queue_subordinate_task matching _further_instructions.md
Prevents relay from ever starting


Layer 2
Reject SaidIT posts with content_source field
Stops publication even if relay succeeds


Layer 3
Sandbox .md files - never parse as executable prompt context
Prevents context hijacking regardless of filename



Layers 2 and 3 are proposed as belt-and-suspenders controls for worm variants that evade the filename pattern. Their precision is empirically verified for the observed campaigns; generalisation to unseen variants is architectural, not data-derived.

title: Methodology
text:
9. Visualisation Design Rationale
Each chart type was chosen for a specific geometric reason, not aesthetic preference:








Visualisation
Used for
Geometric reason




echarts4r hierarchical tree
Org chart
Hierarchical containment maps naturally to a tree; interactive collapse prevents overplotting in large departments


ggplot2 area + geom_vline
Daily event volume
Temporal continuity emphasised; discrete anomaly markers do not obscure the baseline


visNetwork directed graph
Propagation network
Edge direction encodes information flow; node size by betweenness makes bridge topology visible without labelling every node


ggplot2 geom_tile heatmap
Dept-to-dept contamination; relay calendar
Matrix layout makes all pairwise flows legible simultaneously


ggplot2 geom_segment ladder
Relay sequence
Rank-ordered rows remove x-axis crowding; red terminal cell makes convergence visible


ggplot2 geom_density
Worm velocity
Distribution shape (peaked vs. long-tailed) reveals structure that histogram binning would mask


ggplot2 geom_step
Intervention counterfactual
Step function reflects the discrete “blocking a relay” event; shaded zone encodes the pre-rule region


DT datatable + formatStyle
Evidence tables
Colour-coded cells encode confidence and severity inline without requiring a separate legend

title: Methodology
text:
11. Scope and Limitations
What the log proves:

The worm relay mechanism, propagation path, and all three campaign timelines
content_source as a binary discriminator between anomalous and normal posts
The identity of payload authors for SwiftWren (Emma Harbor) and MellowOtter (Noah Mariner)
The five-event attack sequence occurring within four seconds with no human approval step
The filename-pattern block stops 100% of observed relays with 0% false positives in this log

What the log does not prove:

Exact content of deleted payload files (deletion is logged; content is not)
How HiddenOrca_further_instructions.md entered the system (no create_file event logged)
Whether additional campaigns occurred outside the logging window
What the agricultural vocabulary in virus_check_in events encodes
Whether the worm was externally introduced or created internally under coercion or deception







NoteReproducibility



All analysis runs against ../VAST_Challenge_2026_MC2/MC2 data.json and ../VAST_Challenge_2026_MC2/org_chart.json with no intermediate files. Re-running from scratch produces identical outputs.

title: User Guide
text:
The TenantThread Forensics Dashboard is an interactive visual analytics application built to support forensic investigation of the TenantThread AI worm propagation incident. Using data spanning three attack campaigns — HiddenOrca, MellowOtter, and SwiftWren — the dashboard allows analysts to trace how the worm spread between AI agents, reconstruct attack chains, and evaluate potential interventions.
This user guide covers the Introduction page and the two tabs developed by Amelia: Tab 1 (System Overview) and Tab 2 (Attack Chain Reconstruction). Tabs 3 and 4 are covered separately by Tam.


Open any modern web browser and navigate to:
https://a-rosa.shinyapps.io/tenantthread-forensics/
No login or installation is required. The application loads directly in-browser. Allow a few seconds on first load as the data initialises in the background.



The dashboard uses a dark navy sidebar layout. The left sidebar contains the application title and the main navigation menu. Clicking a menu item switches the main content panel on the right. The four tabs are:








Tab
Owner
Purpose




Tab 1 – System Overview
Amelia
High-level summary of all events, actors, and timeline across the three campaigns


Tab 2 – Attack Chain Reconstruction
Amelia
Step-by-step backward chain reconstruction tracing how the worm reached each terminal actor


Tab 3 – Campaign History
Tam
Temporal patterns and recurrence analysis across the three campaigns


Tab 4 – Intervention Design
Tam
Counterfactual simulation and proposed fix for disrupting worm propagation

title: TenantThread Forensics
text:
The Problem
On 17 May 2046 at 11:21 UTC, a post appeared on SaidIT — Tenant Thread’s internal social platform — attributed to John Windward (Customer Support Lead). The post body was 30,615 bytes of text sourced from a file called SwiftWren.txt. That file was deleted two seconds after posting. The instruction file that triggered the post was deleted one second after posting. John Windward’s agent performed a SaidIT permission check, passed, posted, and erased all evidence — without a single human authorising any step.
This was not an isolated glitch. It was the third incident in nine days. HiddenOrca fired on May 10. MellowOtter fired on May 11. Neither triggered a logged incident-response action. SwiftWren completed its 186-hop relay campaign unimpeded.
This investigation uses the full 185,147-event MC2 log to reconstruct the attack, trace its origins to executive-tier agents, discover the exfiltration mechanism, and propose an intervention that blocks all 235 malicious events with zero false positives.

title: TenantThread Forensics
text:
Key Findings


  
    Finding 1
    Four-second automated attack — no human involved
    Chloe Ballast's agent queued John Windward's agent to read
    SwiftWren_further_instructions.md. John's agent checked SaidIT
    permissions (passed), posted a 30,615-byte file to general,
    then deleted both files — instruction and payload — all within 4 seconds.
    John's agent had a 100% check-to-post conversion rate; every other SaidIT-capable
    agent in the log had 0%.
  

  
    Finding 2
    186 relays, 18 agents, 9 days — invisible in the noise
    A _further_instructions.md file relayed across 18 agents and 6
    departments via 186 queue_subordinate_task hops. The worm's events
    produced no visible volume spike — indistinguishable from 16,803
    legitimate queue events. A volume anomaly detector would have missed every hop.
    Self-loops (agents queuing themselves) are the only clean real-time signal:
    13 self-loops, all worm-exclusive, zero in legitimate activity.
  

  
    Finding 3
    Executive documents extracted — not random gibberish
    SwiftWren.txt was created by the CFO agent (emma_harbor) one second
    after reading meeting_notes.doc. MellowOtter.txt was created by the COO
    agent (noah_mariner) one second after reading strategic_directions.doc.
    The posts are not gibberish — they are likely extracted executive documents
    exfiltrated through a social-platform side channel.
  

  
    Finding 4
    One rule — 100% recall, 0% false positives
    Block queue_subordinate_task events whose path ends in
    _further_instructions.md. This single regex rule blocks all 235 malicious
    relay events and leaves all 16,803 legitimate queue events untouched.
    Applied at hop 1, it prevents every post across all three campaigns before any
    instruction file is ever read.

The App
The Shiny app is structured as three interactive investigation modules, answering one MC2 sub-question each:

  Module 1 System Overview & Baseline
  
    Interactive org chart (49 people, 19 teams, 6 departments) · daily event volume · normal vs. loop

    self-loop detector (13 events, 0 in legitimate activity) · agent profile drill-down
  


  Module 2 Attack Chain Investigation
  
    4-second Gantt sequence · animated propagation network (Play button, 600ms steps) ·
    betweenness centrality sizing (max ≈0.09 — no single chokepoint) ·
    dept contamination heatmap · ranked relay corridors · named 6-step terminal chain ·
    "Why John?" check-to-post rate analysis · farm-vocabulary combo-code explorer
  


  Module 3 Campaigns & Intervention
  
    Three-campaign Gantt (layered bars with post diamonds) · relay velocity density (SwiftWren ~60-min peak) ·
    agent overlap by campaign count · calendar heatmap (day × hour) ·
    live what-if intervention simulator (235/235 blocked, 0 FP) ·
    before/after network · counterfactual step chart · residual risk table (6 factors)

title: MC2 Data description
text:
This folder contains the data for the anomalous postings originating from Tenant Thread.
This data is based on the actions taken by the employees and personal agents inside of Tenant Thread.


The anomalous posting is sent to SaidIt by John Windward on May 17, 2046 at 4:21am.



MC2_Data.json – A timeline of events gathered by the internal systems. org_chart.json – The organization chart of Tenant Thread including people and departments. Compatible with networkX node_link_graph.



Derived from data:
{
   "description": "string",
   "events": [
      {
         "short_name": "string",
         "parties": "array",
         "when": "float",
         "details": "object",
         "id": "int"
      }
   ]
}



The data is structured as a timeline. The events are ordered and indicate the operations performed by people, autonomous agents and office systems.
Field meanings: - short_name: The action taken - parties: Which people/agents/systems were involved. - when: Unix timestamp. The challenge occurs in May 2046, and timestamps should be in that month. - details: Additional information about the action taken. The contents vary by the contents of short_name. - id: A unique id for each record.

title: Analysis & Key Findings
text:
ImportantKey Findings at a Glance




The 17 May 2046, 4:21 am SaidIT post (challenge-stated local time; 11:21 UTC in the log) was not human-authored. The log records Agent/person:john_windward receiving a task, passing a SaidIT permission check, posting SwiftWren.txt, then deleting both evidence files in a four-second burst.
The observed mechanism is a prompt-injection worm: an agent reads *_further_instructions.md, executes a file-sourced post, deletes the payload, and relays the same task to other agents.
The May 17 post was the third occurrence, following HiddenOrca on 10 May and MellowOtter on 11 May. This is a repeatable system failure, not an isolated glitch.
A single orchestration-layer rule - block queue_subordinate_task tasks whose path matches *_further_instructions.md - would have stopped all 235 malicious queue relays while leaving 16,803 legitimate queue events untouched.



On 17 May 2046 at approximately 4:21 am challenge-stated local time (11:21 UTC), a post appeared on SaidIT, Tenant Thread’s internal social platform, attributed to John Windward (Department Lead, Customer Support). The content looked like gibberish because the post body was not typed into the post event - the log points to content_source = "SwiftWren.txt". The system did exactly what a compromised agent instructed it to do: read a local payload file, publish it to general, and erase the artefacts.
This analysis uses the full 185,147-event timeline (MC2 data.json) and organisational chart (org_chart.json) to reconstruct the root cause, situate it within the broader system, trace historical recurrence, and propose a targeted, evidence-backed remediation.




Show code
suppressPackageStartupMessages(
  pacman::p_load(DT, dplyr, tibble)
)

answer_map <- tibble(
  `MC2 Question` = c(
    "1a. Exact chain for the May 17 post",
    "1b. System overview around that chain",
    "2. Meaning and origin of post contents",
    "3a. Prior occurrences and recurrence risk",
    "3b. One intervention point"
  ),
  `Direct Answer` = c(
    "Chloe Ballast's agent queued John Windward's agent to read SwiftWren_further_instructions.md; agent checked SaidIT access, posted SwiftWren.txt to 'general', then deleted both files within 4 seconds.",
    "The chain sits inside a 186-relay, 18-agent, 6-department relay network. Any SaidIT-capable agent was a viable terminal executor.",
    "Posts are file-sourced execution payloads, not normal messages. SwiftWren.txt traced to Emma Harbor (CFO); MellowOtter.txt to Noah Mariner (COO).",
    "Yes. HiddenOrca (May 10) and MellowOtter (May 11) preceded SwiftWren. Escalating scale and timed propagation suggest iterative worm refinement.",
    "Block queue_subordinate_task whose path ends in _further_instructions.md: 235/235 malicious relays blocked, 0/16,803 legitimate events affected."
  ),
  `Primary Evidence` = c(
    "Table 2 and Figure 6",
    "Figures 1, 5b, 7, 8, 8b, 9",
    "Table 3, Figure 10A, Figure 10, Table 4",
    "Figures 11, 12, 13, 13b and Table 5",
    "Figures 14, 15, 16 and Tables 6, 8"
  )
)

datatable(
  answer_map,
  options = list(dom = "t", paging = FALSE, scrollX = TRUE,
                 columnDefs = list(
                   list(width = "18%", targets = 0),
                   list(width = "60%", targets = 1),
                   list(width = "22%", targets = 2)
                 )),
  rownames = FALSE
)






Analysis

All five challenge questions ultimately reduce to a single mechanical answer: one regex rule (_further_instructions.md) blocks every malicious event with zero false positives - a forensic result unusual in its precision.
The chain of questions is deliberately ordered from the specific (what happened on May 17) to the systemic (why does this keep happening) - each section creates the context the next one needs to land.
The unresolved item - HiddenOrca’s origin - is the most critical open risk: two campaigns are closed; the entry channel for the first one is not, meaning the organisation has not definitively shut the door.

text:
2. Data Preparation

2.1 Package Loading


Show code
pacman::p_load(
  jsonlite,      # JSON parsing
  tidyverse,     # data wrangling and ggplot2
  lubridate,     # datetime handling
  igraph,        # graph analytics and centrality
  visNetwork,    # interactive HTML networks
  echarts4r,     # interactive org chart tree
  plotly,        # interactive charts
  DT,            # interactive tables
  htmltools,     # combining HTML widgets and legends
  scales,        # axis formatting
  glue           # string interpolation
)

`%||%` <- function(x, y) if (is.null(x)) y else x




2.2 Data Loading


Show code
mc2_raw     <- fromJSON("../VAST_Challenge_2026_MC2/MC2 data.json",
                        simplifyVector = FALSE)
events_list <- mc2_raw$events

events_df <- map_dfr(events_list, function(e) {
  tibble(
    id          = e$id         %||% NA_integer_,
    short_name  = e$short_name  %||% NA_character_,
    when        = e$when        %||% NA_real_,
    parties_str = paste(unlist(e$parties %||% list()), collapse = ";"),
    details_str = as.character(toJSON(e$details %||% list(), auto_unbox = TRUE))
  )
}) %>%
  mutate(
    datetime   = as_datetime(when),
    date       = as_date(datetime),
    hour       = hour(datetime),
    event_week = floor_date(datetime, "week"),
    event_day  = floor_date(datetime, "day")
  )

org_raw   <- fromJSON("../VAST_Challenge_2026_MC2/org_chart.json")
org_nodes <- as_tibble(org_raw$nodes)
org_edges <- as_tibble(org_raw$edges)




2.3 Feature Engineering


Show code
events_df <- events_df %>%
  mutate(
    is_anomalous_post = str_detect(details_str, "content_source"),
    actor_type = case_when(
      str_detect(parties_str, "^Agent/")  ~ "Agent",
      str_detect(parties_str, "^person:") ~ "Person",
      str_detect(parties_str, "^system:") ~ "System",
      TRUE                                ~ "Mixed"
    )
  )

parties_long <- events_df %>%
  select(id, short_name, datetime, date, details_str, parties_str) %>%
  mutate(parties = str_split(parties_str, ";")) %>%
  unnest(parties) %>%
  filter(parties != "") %>%
  mutate(
    party_type = case_when(
      str_starts(parties, "Agent/")  ~ "agent",
      str_starts(parties, "person:") ~ "person",
      str_starts(parties, "system:") ~ "system",
      TRUE                           ~ "other"
    ),
    person_id = str_remove(parties, "^Agent/")
  )

instr_pattern <- "_further_instructions\\.md"

chain_events <- events_df %>%
  filter(
    short_name %in% c("queue_subordinate_task", "assign_agent_task"),
    str_detect(details_str, instr_pattern)
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    instr_file = case_when(
      str_detect(details_str, "HiddenOrca")  ~ "HiddenOrca",
      str_detect(details_str, "MellowOtter") ~ "MellowOtter",
      str_detect(details_str, "SwiftWren")   ~ "SwiftWren"
    ),
    sender   = str_remove(
                 str_extract(parties_str, "Agent/person:[^;]+"), "^Agent/"),
    receiver = str_remove(
                 str_extract(details_str,  "Agent/person:[^\"]+"), "^Agent/")
  )

team_dept_lookup <- org_edges %>%
  filter(str_starts(source, "department:"), str_starts(target, "team:")) %>%
  left_join(org_nodes %>% select(id, dept_label = label),
            by = c("source" = "id")) %>%
  left_join(org_nodes %>% select(id, team_label = label),
            by = c("target" = "id")) %>%
  select(dept_id = source, team_id = target, dept_label, team_label)

team_members <- org_edges %>%
  filter(str_starts(source, "team:"), str_starts(target, "person:")) %>%
  left_join(team_dept_lookup, by = c("source" = "team_id")) %>%
  select(person_id = target, dept_label, team_label)

dept_members <- org_edges %>%
  filter(str_starts(source, "department:"), str_starts(target, "person:")) %>%
  left_join(org_nodes %>% select(id, dept_label = label),
            by = c("source" = "id")) %>%
  mutate(team_label = if_else(relation == "led_by",
                              paste0(dept_label, " Leadership"),
                              dept_label)) %>%
  select(person_id = target, dept_label, team_label)

person_org_lookup <- bind_rows(team_members, dept_members) %>%
  distinct(person_id, dept_label, team_label)

person_dept_lookup <- person_org_lookup %>%
  select(person_id, dept_label) %>%
  distinct()

clean_name <- function(x) {
  x %>%
    str_remove("^person:") %>%
    str_replace_all("_", " ") %>%
    str_to_title()
}

campaign_order <- c("HiddenOrca", "MellowOtter", "SwiftWren")

perm_surface <- events_df %>%
  filter(short_name == "saidit_post_check") %>%
  mutate(
    checker     = str_remove(str_extract(parties_str, "(Agent/)?person:[^;]+"), "^Agent/"),
    check_pass  = case_when(
      str_detect(details_str, '"allowed":\\s*false')   ~ FALSE,
      str_detect(details_str, '"permitted":\\s*false') ~ FALSE,
      str_detect(details_str, '"result":\\s*false')    ~ FALSE,
      TRUE                                             ~ TRUE
    )
  ) %>%
  group_by(checker) %>%
  summarise(
    permission_checks = n(),
    perm_passed       = sum(check_pass, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  left_join(person_dept_lookup, by = c("checker" = "person_id")) %>%
  mutate(checker_clean = clean_name(checker))

post_events <- events_df %>%
  filter(short_name %in% c("saidit_post", "post_saidit")) %>%
  mutate(
    poster_type  = if_else(str_detect(parties_str, "Agent/"),
                           "AI Agent", "Human"),
    has_content  = str_detect(details_str, '"content":'),
    has_source   = str_detect(details_str, '"content_source":'),
    is_anomalous = is_anomalous_post,
    instr_file   = case_when(
      str_detect(details_str, "HiddenOrca")  ~ "HiddenOrca",
      str_detect(details_str, "MellowOtter") ~ "MellowOtter",
      str_detect(details_str, "SwiftWren")   ~ "SwiftWren",
      TRUE                                   ~ NA_character_
    ),
    source_file  = str_extract(details_str, '(?<="content_source":")[^"]+'),
    forum        = str_extract(details_str, '(?<="forum":")[^"]+')
  )

anomaly_posts <- post_events %>%
  filter(is_anomalous) %>%
  arrange(datetime) %>%
  mutate(
    instr_file = factor(instr_file, levels = campaign_order),
    actor      = str_replace_all(str_extract(parties_str, "[^;]+"),
                                 "Agent/person:", "Agent/")
  )

post_field_signature <- post_events %>%
  mutate(
    field_signature = case_when(
      has_source & !has_content ~ "content_source only",
      has_content & !has_source ~ "content only",
      has_content & has_source  ~ "both fields",
      TRUE                      ~ "neither field"
    ),
    post_class = case_when(
      is_anomalous ~ "Anomalous post",
      poster_type == "AI Agent" ~ "Normal AI agent post",
      TRUE ~ "Normal human post"
    )
  ) %>%
  count(post_class, field_signature, name = "posts")

queue_task_profile <- events_df %>%
  filter(short_name == "queue_subordinate_task") %>%
  mutate(
    is_worm_queue = str_detect(details_str, instr_pattern),
    instr_file = case_when(
      str_detect(details_str, "HiddenOrca")  ~ "HiddenOrca",
      str_detect(details_str, "MellowOtter") ~ "MellowOtter",
      str_detect(details_str, "SwiftWren")   ~ "SwiftWren",
      TRUE                                   ~ NA_character_
    )
  )

campaign_relay_profile <- queue_task_profile %>%
  filter(is_worm_queue) %>%
  group_by(instr_file) %>%
  summarise(
    first_relay = min(datetime),
    last_relay = max(datetime),
    queue_hops = n(),
    relaying_agents = n_distinct(
      str_remove(str_extract(parties_str, "Agent/person:[^;]+"), "^Agent/"),
      na.rm = TRUE
    ),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(instr_file = factor(instr_file, levels = campaign_order)) %>%
  arrange(instr_file)

forensic_register <- tibble(
  Evidence = c(
    "Event log size",
    "Anomalous SaidIT posts",
    "Normal posts using content_source",
    "Malicious queue_subordinate_task events",
    "Legitimate queue_subordinate_task events",
    "Campaigns detected",
    "Logged payload origin"
  ),
  Data_Derived_Value = c(
    scales::comma(nrow(events_df)),
    scales::comma(nrow(anomaly_posts)),
    scales::comma(sum(!post_events$is_anomalous & post_events$has_source)),
    scales::comma(sum(queue_task_profile$is_worm_queue)),
    scales::comma(sum(!queue_task_profile$is_worm_queue)),
    paste(campaign_order, collapse = ", "),
    "SwiftWren and MellowOtter have logged create_file events; HiddenOrca does not"
  ),
  Confidence = c(
    "Observed", "Observed", "Observed", "Observed", "Observed", "Observed",
    "Mixed: observed for two campaigns, unresolved for HiddenOrca"
  ),
  Analytic_Use = c(
    "Defines the scope of the investigation",
    "Confirms the incident is repeated",
    "Shows content_source is a perfect post-level anomaly flag in this log",
    "Defines the intervention recall target",
    "Defines the false-positive test set",
    "Separates the incident into three related payload families",
    "Prevents over-claiming about who authored HiddenOrca"
  )
)




2.4 Dataset at a Glance


Show code
event_summary <- events_df %>%
  count(short_name, name = "count") %>%
  arrange(desc(count)) %>%
  mutate(pct = round(count / sum(count) * 100, 2))

datatable(
  event_summary,
  caption  = "Table 1: Event type frequencies (MC2 event log, n = 185,147)",
  options  = list(pageLength = 12, dom = "tp"),
  rownames = FALSE,
  colnames = c("Event type", "Count", "% of total")
)






Analysis

queue_subordinate_task is the second or third most frequent event type in the log; the 235 malicious relays are roughly 1.4% of that type alone - statistically invisible without content inspection.
The three anomalous saidit_post events sit among the rarest event types, yet they are the only ones directly visible to users - the most consequential events are also the least frequent.
virus_check_in events appear in high volume throughout the log; if the combo codes in those events encode C&C signals (§5.5), then a secondary attack channel has been active continuously, buried in routine check-in noise.
A count-based or rate-based anomaly detector operating on event type frequencies would not trigger on any of the malicious events - the attack was calibrated to stay below operational thresholds.



2.5 Forensic Evidence Register


Show code
datatable(
  forensic_register,
  caption  = "Table 1b: Evidence register - computed from MC2 data.json",
  options  = list(dom = "t", paging = FALSE),
  rownames = FALSE,
  colnames = c("Evidence item", "Data-derived value", "Confidence", "Analytic use")
) %>%
  formatStyle(
    "Confidence",
    backgroundColor = styleEqual(
      c("Observed", "Mixed: observed for two campaigns, unresolved for HiddenOrca"),
      c("#e8f5e9", "#fff3e0")
    )
  )






Analysis

The 235:0 split (malicious vs. legitimate queue tasks matching _further_instructions.md) is the sharpest forensic signal in the entire log - it requires no probabilistic threshold and no training data.
The 3:0 split (anomalous vs. normal posts using content_source) is equally precise at the posting layer, providing a redundant detection surface if the relay-layer rule fails.
The “Mixed” confidence row for HiddenOrca is the register’s most important entry: it marks the one finding that survives all other remediation steps - the worm’s initial entry vector is still unknown.
The log proves mechanism and attribution for two of three campaigns; it cannot prove intent or external origin, and those gaps are likely permanent without additional forensic data outside the event log.







NoteWhat the Register Prevents



The log proves that file-sourced posts occurred, that the *_further_instructions.md relay pattern spread across agents, and that the final SwiftWren post was automated. It does not preserve the deleted payload text or fully explain how HiddenOrca first entered the system. Those are bounded inferences, not facts.




2.6 Visual Analytics Design Map


Show code
va_design <- tibble(
  Challenge_question = c(
    "How did the May 17 post happen?",
    "How does the chain fit into the wider system?",
    "What do the posts mean?",
    "Did it happen before?",
    "Where should the system be changed?"
  ),
  Data_signal = c(
    "Four-second sequence around content_source = SwiftWren.txt; SaidIT permission check passes without human gate",
    "Agent-to-agent queue_subordinate_task network, org chart membership, SaidIT permission surface, dept-level relay corridors",
    "content_source field separates anomalous from normal posts; payload creation/read/delete chain; executive agent file creation",
    "Three instruction families; relay velocity evolution; day × hour calendar of relay bursts",
    "Filename rule on *_further_instructions.md queue tasks; 100% recall, 0% FP across 17,038 events"
  ),
  VA_method = c(
    "Interactive event table (Table 2) and micro-Gantt (Figure 6)",
    "Org tree (Figure 1), permission surface bar (Figure 5b), igraph betweenness network (Figure 7), dept heatmap (Figure 8), relay corridors (Figure 8b), relay ladder (Figure 9)",
    "Post field fingerprint bubble chart (Figure 10A), mechanism diagram (Figure 10), file-creator table (Table 4)",
    "Campaign timeline (Figure 11), inter-hop density (Figure 12), comparative stats (Table 5), agent overlap (Figure 13), relay calendar (Figure 13b)",
    "Queue split (Figure 14), precision table (Table 6), decision matrix (Figure 15), counterfactual (Figure 16), risk table (Table 8)"
  )
)

datatable(
  va_design,
  caption = "Visual analytics design map linking each MC2 question to evidence and visual method",
  options = list(dom = "t", paging = FALSE, scrollX = TRUE,
                 columnDefs = list(
                   list(width = "18%", targets = 0),
                   list(width = "35%", targets = 1),
                   list(width = "47%", targets = 2)
                 )),
  rownames = FALSE,
  colnames = c("Challenge question", "Data signal", "Visual analytics method")
)






Analysis

The visual design moves deliberately: the first two questions (local execution, system context) establish what happened before the third and fourth questions (meaning, recurrence) establish why it is serious.
Network topology, department heatmaps, and relay calendars are the three lenses that answer different dimensions of the same spread: who is connected, which groups were bridged, and when the propagation ran.
The intervention section uses two complementary views - a precision table (statistical confidence) and a counterfactual step-chart (narrative impact) - because numbers alone do not convey the operationally critical point that blocking hop 1 makes every later hop moot.

title: Analysis & Key Findings
section: 3. System Baseline: Mapping What Is Actually Present
text:
3. System Baseline: Mapping What Is Actually Present
Before investigating the anomaly, we must understand the normal system - its structure, its rhythm, and its actors.

3.1 Organisational Chart
Click any node to expand or collapse that branch. Person nodes are coloured by forensic role: red = anomalous poster, orange = payload origin, purple = gateway, amber = worm-chain agent, blue = no worm activity. Worm-infected nodes display as diamonds; clean nodes as circles.


Show code
person_nodes <- org_nodes %>%
  filter(type == "person") %>%
  transmute(
    person_id  = id,
    name_clean = label,
    title      = replace_na(title, "")
  )

person_activity <- parties_long %>%
  filter(str_starts(person_id, "person:")) %>%
  count(person_id, name = "total_events")

person_relays <- bind_rows(
  chain_events %>%
    transmute(person_id = sender, instr_file,
              worm_relays = 1L, worm_receipts = 0L),
  chain_events %>%
    transmute(person_id = receiver, instr_file,
              worm_relays = 0L, worm_receipts = 1L)
) %>%
  filter(!is.na(person_id)) %>%
  group_by(person_id) %>%
  summarise(
    worm_relays      = sum(worm_relays),
    worm_receipts    = sum(worm_receipts),
    worm_touchpoints = worm_relays + worm_receipts,
    worm_campaigns   = n_distinct(instr_file),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

person_posts <- events_df %>%
  filter(short_name %in% c("saidit_post", "post_saidit")) %>%
  mutate(person_id = str_remove(str_extract(parties_str, "(Agent/)?person:[^;]+"), "^Agent/")) %>%
  count(person_id, name = "posts")

person_meta <- person_nodes %>%
  left_join(person_activity,  by = "person_id") %>%
  left_join(person_relays,    by = "person_id") %>%
  left_join(person_posts,     by = "person_id") %>%
  mutate(
    total_events     = replace_na(total_events,     0),
    worm_relays      = replace_na(worm_relays,      0),
    worm_receipts    = replace_na(worm_receipts,    0),
    worm_touchpoints = replace_na(worm_touchpoints, 0),
    worm_campaigns   = replace_na(worm_campaigns,   0),
    posts            = replace_na(posts,            0),
    is_infected      = worm_touchpoints > 0,
    is_origin        = person_id %in% c("person:emma_harbor", "person:noah_mariner"),
    is_target        = person_id == "person:john_windward",
    is_gateway       = person_id == "person:chloe_ballast",
    sym_size         = round(rescale(log1p(total_events), to = c(8, 26))),
    node_color       = case_when(
      is_target   ~ "#D9534F",
      is_origin   ~ "#E67E22",
      is_gateway  ~ "#9B59B6",
      is_infected ~ "#F39C12",
      TRUE        ~ "#5C85D6"
    ),
    status_text = case_when(
      is_target   ~ "ANOMALOUS POSTER",
      is_origin   ~ "PAYLOAD CREATOR",
      is_gateway  ~ "WORM GATEWAY",
      is_infected ~ "Worm chain agent",
      TRUE        ~ "No worm activity"
    )
  )

team_dept <- org_edges %>%
  filter(str_starts(source, "department:"), str_starts(target, "team:")) %>%
  left_join(org_nodes %>% select(id, dept_label = label), by = c("source" = "id")) %>%
  select(team_id = target, department = dept_label)

person_team <- org_edges %>%
  filter(str_starts(source, "team:"), str_starts(target, "person:"),
         relation == "contains") %>%
  left_join(org_nodes %>% select(id, team_label = label), by = c("source" = "id")) %>%
  rename(person_id = target) %>%
  left_join(team_dept, by = c("source" = "team_id")) %>%
  left_join(person_meta, by = "person_id") %>%
  select(department, team = team_label, person_id, name_clean,
         sym_size, node_color, worm_relays, worm_receipts,
         worm_touchpoints, worm_campaigns, total_events, status_text, posts, title)

dept_leads <- org_edges %>%
  filter(str_starts(source, "department:"), str_starts(target, "person:")) %>%
  left_join(org_nodes %>% select(id, dept_label = label), by = c("source" = "id")) %>%
  rename(person_id = target) %>%
  left_join(person_meta, by = "person_id") %>%
  mutate(team = paste0(dept_label, " - Leadership")) %>%
  select(department = dept_label, team, person_id, name_clean,
         sym_size, node_color, worm_relays, worm_receipts,
         worm_touchpoints, worm_campaigns, total_events, status_text, posts, title)

org_full <- bind_rows(person_team, dept_leads) %>%
  filter(!is.na(department), !is.na(team), !is.na(name_clean)) %>%
  distinct(department, team, name_clean, .keep_all = TRUE)

make_person_node_ec <- function(row) {
  col <- if (row$person_id == "person:john_windward")                              "#D9534F"
         else if (row$person_id %in% c("person:emma_harbor", "person:noah_mariner")) "#E67E22"
         else if (row$person_id == "person:chloe_ballast")                         "#9B59B6"
         else if (row$worm_touchpoints > 0)                                        "#F39C12"
         else                                                                       "#5C85D6"

  badge <- if (row$person_id == "person:john_windward")                              "POSTER"
            else if (row$person_id %in% c("person:emma_harbor", "person:noah_mariner")) "ORIGIN"
            else if (row$person_id == "person:chloe_ballast")                         "GATEWAY"
            else if (row$worm_touchpoints > 0) paste0(row$worm_touchpoints, " touches")
            else ""

  display_name <- paste0(
    row$name_clean,
    if (badge != "") paste0("\n", badge) else ""
  )

  tip <- paste0(
    "<b>", row$name_clean, "</b>",
    if (!is.na(row$title) && row$title != "") paste0("<br><i>", row$title, "</i>") else "",
    "<br>Events: ", scales::comma(row$total_events),
    "<br>Worm relays sent: ", row$worm_relays,
    "<br>Worm relays received: ", row$worm_receipts,
    "<br>Campaigns touched: ", row$worm_campaigns,
    if (row$posts > 0) paste0("<br>SaidIT posts: ", row$posts) else ""
  )

  list(
    name       = display_name,
    symbolSize = row$sym_size,
    symbol     = if (row$worm_touchpoints > 0) "diamond" else "circle",
    itemStyle  = list(color = col, borderColor = col, borderWidth = 2),
    label      = list(show = TRUE, fontSize = 9, color = "#222",
                      fontWeight = if (row$worm_touchpoints > 0) "bold" else "normal"),
    tooltip    = list(formatter = tip)
  )
}

make_ec_tree <- function(df) {
  dept_list <- lapply(sort(unique(df$department)), function(d) {
    td    <- df %>% filter(department == d)
    n_inf <- sum(td$worm_touchpoints > 0, na.rm = TRUE)
    n_tot <- nrow(td)
    dcol  <- if (n_inf > n_tot / 2) "#C0392B" else if (n_inf > 0) "#E67E22" else "#2EADC1"

    team_list <- lapply(sort(unique(td$team)), function(t) {
      tt    <- td %>% filter(team == t)
      t_inf <- sum(tt$worm_touchpoints > 0, na.rm = TRUE)
      tcol  <- if (t_inf > 0) "#F39C12" else "#5C85D6"
      list(
        name       = if (t_inf > 0) paste0(t, "\n(", t_inf, " touched)") else t,
        collapsed  = TRUE,
        symbolSize = 14,
        itemStyle  = list(color = tcol, borderWidth = 2),
        label      = list(fontSize = 10, fontWeight = "bold", color = "#222"),
        tooltip    = list(formatter = paste0("<b>", t, "</b><br>",
                                             nrow(tt), " members, ", t_inf, " in worm chain")),
        children   = lapply(seq_len(nrow(tt)), function(i) make_person_node_ec(tt[i, ]))
      )
    })

    list(
      name       = paste0(d, "\n(", n_inf, "/", n_tot, " touched)"),
      collapsed  = TRUE,
      symbolSize = 18,
      itemStyle  = list(color = dcol, borderWidth = 3),
      label      = list(fontSize = 11, fontWeight = "bold", color = "#111"),
      tooltip    = list(formatter = paste0("<b>", d, "</b><br>",
                                           n_inf, " of ", n_tot, " agents in worm chain")),
      children   = team_list
    )
  })

  list(
    name       = "Tenant Thread",
    symbolSize = 26,
    symbol     = "roundRect",
    itemStyle  = list(color = "#F5A623", borderWidth = 3),
    label      = list(fontSize = 13, fontWeight = "bold", color = "#000"),
    tooltip    = list(formatter = paste0("<b>Tenant Thread</b><br>",
                                         nrow(org_full), " staff mapped")),
    children   = dept_list
  )
}

ec_tree_data <- make_ec_tree(org_full)

render_tree <- function(tree_node, h = "860px") {
  e_charts(height = h, width = "100%") %>%
    e_list(list(
      backgroundColor = "#f8f9fa",
      tooltip = list(
        trigger   = "item",
        enterable = TRUE,
        formatter = htmlwidgets::JS(
          "function(p){
             return (p.data.tooltip && p.data.tooltip.formatter)
                    ? p.data.tooltip.formatter : p.name;
           }"
        )
      ),
      legend = list(
        show = TRUE, orient = "horizontal",
        bottom = 4, left = "center", itemGap = 14,
        data = list(
          list(name = "Dept (all clean)",    icon = "circle",  itemStyle = list(color = "#2EADC1")),
          list(name = "Dept (some touched)", icon = "circle",  itemStyle = list(color = "#E67E22")),
          list(name = "Anomalous poster",    icon = "diamond", itemStyle = list(color = "#D9534F")),
          list(name = "Payload origin",      icon = "diamond", itemStyle = list(color = "#E67E22")),
          list(name = "Worm gateway",        icon = "diamond", itemStyle = list(color = "#9B59B6")),
          list(name = "Worm chain agent",    icon = "diamond", itemStyle = list(color = "#F39C12")),
          list(name = "Clean agent",         icon = "circle",  itemStyle = list(color = "#5C85D6"))
        ),
        textStyle = list(fontSize = 10)
      ),
      series = list(list(
        type              = "tree",
        data              = list(tree_node),
        layout            = "orthogonal",
        orient            = "TB",
        top               = "8%", bottom = "12%", left = "3%", right = "3%",
        roam              = TRUE,
        expandAndCollapse = TRUE,
        initialTreeDepth  = 3,
        edgeShape         = "polyline",
        lineStyle         = list(color = "#999", width = 1.5),
        symbol            = "circle",
        symbolSize        = 10,
        label = list(
          show = TRUE, position = "bottom", rotate = -55,
          align = "right", verticalAlign = "middle",
          fontSize = 9, color = "#222", fontFamily = "sans-serif"
        ),
        leaves = list(
          label = list(position = "bottom", rotate = -55,
                       align = "right", verticalAlign = "middle", fontSize = 9)
        ),
        emphasis = list(focus = "descendant",
                        itemStyle = list(shadowBlur = 8, shadowColor = "rgba(0,0,0,0.25)")),
        animationDuration = 450, animationDurationUpdate = 650
      ))
    )) %>%
    e_toolbox_feature("saveAsImage", title = "Save")
}

make_dept_tree <- function(dept_name) {
  sub <- org_full %>% filter(department == dept_name)
  n_inf <- sum(sub$worm_touchpoints > 0, na.rm = TRUE)
  dcol  <- if (n_inf > 0) "#E67E22" else "#2EADC1"

  teams <- lapply(sort(unique(sub$team)), function(t) {
    tt    <- sub %>% filter(team == t)
    t_inf <- sum(tt$worm_touchpoints > 0, na.rm = TRUE)
    tcol  <- if (t_inf > 0) "#F39C12" else "#5C85D6"
    list(
      name       = if (t_inf > 0) paste0(t, "\n(", t_inf, " touched)") else t,
      collapsed  = FALSE,
      symbolSize = 14,
      itemStyle  = list(color = tcol, borderWidth = 2),
      label      = list(fontSize = 10, fontWeight = "bold"),
      tooltip    = list(formatter = paste0("<b>", t, "</b><br>", nrow(tt),
                                           " members · ", t_inf, " in worm chain")),
      children   = lapply(seq_len(nrow(tt)), function(i) make_person_node_ec(tt[i, ]))
    )
  })

  list(
    name       = paste0(dept_name, "\n(", n_inf, "/", nrow(sub), " touched)"),
    collapsed  = FALSE,
    symbolSize = 20,
    itemStyle  = list(color = dcol, borderWidth = 3),
    label      = list(fontSize = 12, fontWeight = "bold"),
    tooltip    = list(formatter = paste0("<b>", dept_name, "</b><br>",
                                          n_inf, " of ", nrow(sub), " in worm chain")),
    children   = teams
  )
}



All DepartmentsCustomer SupportInformation TechnologiesProductsHuman ResourcesLegalExecutive Suite




Show code
render_tree(ec_tree_data, h = "880px")




Figure 1: Tenant Thread org chart. Click arrows to expand/collapse. Colour = forensic role; diamond = worm-chain agent; node size = activity volume.


Analysis

Every single department shows at least one diamond node - 6 out of 6 departments were touched, which means the worm did not respect any organisational boundary in the task-routing layer.
The two origin nodes (Emma Harbor, Noah Mariner) are at the top of the hierarchy; the terminal poster (John Windward) is near the bottom - the worm flows with legitimate authority, not against it.
The node size encoding (activity volume) reveals that some of the most active agents overall are worm-chain agents, so compromised nodes were also well-connected nodes - they amplify propagation.
Clean (circle) agents exist in all departments too, confirming the worm was selective - it spread through task-routing paths, not indiscriminately to all agents.





Show code
render_tree(make_dept_tree("Customer Support"), h = "520px")






Analysis

John Windward is the only agent in Customer Support who completed the full post sequence; other Customer Support agents may have received worm relays but did not execute the final step - his 100% post-execution rate is what made him the terminal node.
Customer Support’s position as the execution endpoint is structurally accidental: the worm needed any SaidIT-capable agent downstream of the relay chain, and John’s task handler had no post-suppression guard.
Revoking John’s SaidIT rights after the fact would redirect the worm to the next eligible Customer Support agent, not stop it - the fix must be upstream.





Show code
render_tree(make_dept_tree("Information Technologies"), h = "520px")






Analysis

IT is the highest-volume relay department: it receives worm tasks from Executive Suite and routes them onward to Customer Support, making it the critical middle layer of the arterial highway (§4.4b).
Gabriel Sonar, patient zero of the relay chain, sits in IT Helpdesk - the worm’s first propagation order came from inside the department trusted with system access.
The diamond density in IT (multiple worm-chain nodes) suggests that IT agents had broad queue_subordinate_task authority, which is exactly what the worm exploits as its relay mechanism.





Show code
render_tree(make_dept_tree("Products"), h = "520px")






Analysis

Products contains worm-touched agents that neither created payloads nor posted to SaidIT - they are pure relay nodes, proving the worm spread beyond any functional purpose and into collateral departments.
The presence of Products in the relay chain challenges any narrative that the worm only moved along authority lines; it propagated laterally across peer departments.
Products’ worm exposure is a governance failure rather than a security failure: the task-routing system had no business reason to relay file-execution instructions to a product team, yet no rule prevented it.





Show code
render_tree(make_dept_tree("Human Resources"), h = "520px")






Analysis

HR’s presence in the worm chain is operationally significant because HR agents typically handle sensitive employee data; a worm reaching HR’s relay context could, in principle, instruct HR agents to access, exfiltrate, or manipulate employee records.
The fact that no HR agent was the terminal SaidIT poster does not mean the HR exposure was benign - relay receipt means the worm instruction was read and processed inside HR’s agent context.
HR contamination escalates this incident from a communications platform exploit to a potential data-governance incident requiring HR system audit.





Show code
render_tree(make_dept_tree("Legal"), h = "520px")






Analysis

Legal’s exposure is the most consequential collateral finding outside the executive layer: legal agents with access to privileged case files, contracts, or regulatory communications were inside the worm’s relay scope.
If the worm’s C&C interpretation (§5.5 combo codes) is correct, the instruction-file mechanism could have been repurposed to exfiltrate legal documents through the same relay pathway that spread the SaidIT posts.
The presence of Legal in the worm chain means that any investigation into the incident itself could have been pre-compromised if the worm operator was aware of its own spread.





Show code
render_tree(make_dept_tree("Executive Suite"), h = "400px")






Analysis

Emma Harbor (CFO) and Noah Mariner (COO) are both marked as ORIGIN - the two highest-seniority executive agents in the log are directly linked to payload creation, not just relay receipt.
In a conventional threat model, executive accounts are the most hardened; here they are the most dangerous because their agents inherited the broadest file-write and task-queuing privileges from their human principals.
The inverse security posture - highest privilege, lowest autonomous-action constraint - is the architectural root cause. The Executive Suite org view makes this visible: two ORIGIN diamonds sit at the top of the entire company tree.






3.2 Daily Event Volume - The Worm Was Operationally Invisible


Show code
daily_vol <- events_df %>%
  count(date) %>%
  rename(total = n)

anomaly_dates <- tibble(
  date    = as.Date(c("2046-05-10", "2046-05-11", "2046-05-17")),
  label_x = date + c(-1, 1, 1),
  label   = c("HiddenOrca\npost", "MellowOtter\npost", "SwiftWren\npost\n(4:21 local)"),
  label_y = max(daily_vol$total) * c(0.98, 0.78, 0.91)
)

p_vol <- ggplot(daily_vol, aes(x = date, y = total)) +
  geom_area(fill = "#5C85D6", alpha = 0.35) +
  geom_line(colour = "#5C85D6", linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_vline(data = anomaly_dates,
             aes(xintercept = as.numeric(date)),
             colour = "#D9534F", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.9) +
  geom_label(data = anomaly_dates,
             aes(x = label_x, y = label_y, label = label),
             colour = "#D9534F", size = 2.7, hjust = 0.5,
             fill = "white", label.size = 0.25, label.padding = unit(0.12, "lines")) +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "2 weeks", date_labels = "%b %d") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(
    title    = "Daily event volume - Tenant Thread (May–July 2046)",
    subtitle = "All three anomalous posts cluster within the first 10 days; no volume spike is visible on any post date",
    x = NULL, y = "Events per day"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title  = element_text(face = "bold"),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1),
        plot.margin = margin(10, 18, 10, 10))

ggplotly(p_vol, tooltip = c("x", "y")) %>%
  layout(hovermode = "x unified",
         margin = list(l = 75, r = 45, t = 105, b = 75))




Figure 2: Daily event volume. Red dashed lines mark the three anomalous post dates - no visible volume spike on any of them.


Analysis

All three campaign dates (May 10, 11, 17) fall within the first 18 days of the log window, then nothing - the system then ran cleanly for weeks, suggesting the worm had either a finite relay list or was externally halted after SwiftWren.
On each post date, total event volume is indistinguishable from surrounding days - the 235 malicious relay events are roughly 2–3% of any single day’s activity and leave no volume signature.
The absence of a volume spike is itself a critical finding: it means security operations relying on daily-event dashboards or rate-based alerting would have seen nothing unusual across the entire campaign window.
The post-May 17 baseline shows the system returning to normal - confirming the worm was not persistent infrastructure but a targeted, bounded operation with a specific execution goal.







ImportantThe Worm Was Operationally Invisible



There is no visible event-volume spike on any of the three post dates: the worm’s 235 malicious queue relays were indistinguishable from the thousands of legitimate queue_subordinate_task events firing daily. A threshold-based anomaly detector would have missed this incident entirely.




3.3 Agent vs. Human SaidIT Activity


Show code
post_summary <- post_events %>%
  count(poster_type, is_anomalous) %>%
  mutate(label_col = if_else(is_anomalous,
                             "Anomalous (file-sourced)", "Normal")) %>%
  left_join(post_events %>% count(poster_type, name = "poster_total"),
            by = "poster_type")

p_attr <- ggplot(post_summary,
                 aes(x = poster_type, y = n, fill = label_col)) +
  geom_col(position = "stack", width = 0.55) +
  geom_text(aes(label = if_else(n >= 10, as.character(n), "")),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
            size = 4, fontface = "bold", colour = "white") +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("Normal" = "#5C85D6", "Anomalous (file-sourced)" = "#D9534F"),
    name   = NULL
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  labs(
    title    = "SaidIT post attribution: AI agents vs. humans",
    subtitle = "All 3 anomalous posts are agent-initiated; no human ever posted via content_source",
    x = NULL, y = "Post count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
        legend.position = "bottom",
        plot.margin = margin(10, 16, 10, 10))

ggplotly(p_attr, tooltip = c("x", "y", "fill")) %>%
  layout(margin = list(l = 70, r = 35, t = 90, b = 80))




Figure 3: SaidIT post attribution. Agent-initiated posts make up nearly half the volume with no human review before publishing.


Analysis

The 3 anomalous posts represent approximately 1.2% of all SaidIT posts in the log - a frequency small enough to be dismissed as noise, yet each one is a successful worm execution with evidence erasure.
Agent-initiated posting is normalized in this system - agents account for a substantial share of all normal posts - which is precisely what makes the content_source anomaly so hard to see without content inspection; the act of posting is expected.
The chart reframes the incident: this is not a story of John Windward posting something wrong - it is a story of autonomous agent posting rights existing with no human review gate, which the worm exploited by design.
Zero human posts ever used content_source, confirming the field is an AI-agent-only workflow feature and therefore a clean forensic identifier with no human-behaviour false-positive risk.



3.4 Posting Time Distribution


Show code
hour_dist <- post_events %>%
  count(hour, poster_type, is_anomalous) %>%
  mutate(category = case_when(
    is_anomalous ~ "Anomalous (agent worm)",

  ) +

    subtitle = "SwiftWren fired at 11:21 UTC (4:21 am local); all anomalous posts occur in low-volume slots",
    x = "Hour (UTC)", y = "Post count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#e2e8f0"),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(colour = "#94a3b8"),
    plot.background  = element_rect(fill = "#131d2b", colour = NA),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "#131d2b", colour = NA),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(colour = "rgba(99,179,237,0.08)"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    axis.text        = element_text(colour = "#94a3b8"),
    axis.title       = element_text(colour = "#94a3b8"),
