Figure 4: Hour-of-day distribution of SaidIT posts. Normal human posts spread across business hours; anomalous posts occur at atypical hours.


Analysis

Human posts cluster heavily in mid-day UTC hours consistent with office hours; the distribution is essentially absent before ~06:00 UTC and after ~20:00 UTC - exactly the window where all three anomalous posts fired.
SwiftWren at 11:21 UTC (4:21 am local) is a pre-dawn off-hours slot with near-zero normal human activity; HiddenOrca and MellowOtter also post outside peak hours.
The worm’s timing appears deliberate: posting during low-human-oversight windows reduces the probability of a colleague noticing the post and raising an alert before the evidence files are deleted.
An off-hours agent-post alerting rule (Priority 5 in the supplementary hardening table) would have flagged all three anomalous posts at delivery time - a low-effort control that would have at minimum detected each incident within minutes of execution.



3.5 Weekly Event-Type Heatmap


Show code
top_events <- events_df %>%
  count(short_name) %>%
  slice_max(n, n = 10) %>%
  pull(short_name)

weekly_heat <- events_df %>%
  filter(short_name %in% top_events) %>%
  count(event_week, short_name) %>%
  mutate(short_name = fct_reorder(short_name, n, .fun = sum))

ggplot(weekly_heat, aes(x = event_week, y = short_name, fill = n)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 0.3) +
  scale_fill_gradientn(
    colours = c("#f0f4ff", "#5C85D6", "#1a3a6b"),
    labels  = comma
  ) +
  scale_x_datetime(date_breaks = "2 weeks", date_labels = "%b %d") +
  labs(
    title = "Weekly event-type heatmap - top 10 event types",
    x = NULL, y = NULL, fill = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1),
        plot.title  = element_text(face = "bold"))





Figure 5: Weekly event-type heatmap. The early-May spike in queue_subordinate_task is the worm’s propagation window - retrospectively visible but not alarming in real time.




Analysis

The early-May cell for queue_subordinate_task is only marginally darker than surrounding weeks - the 235 malicious relays appear as a barely perceptible bump against the weekly baseline of thousands of legitimate queue events.
virus_check_in (where the combo codes live) shows as a consistent, high-density band across every week - this continuous signal would require content-level inspection, not volume monitoring, to distinguish suspicious from benign.
Crucially, the heatmap shows normal operations resuming immediately after the May 17 post - the worm had a bounded execution window, not an indefinite residency, which is consistent with a targeted operation rather than persistent malware.
A retrospective analyst seeing this heatmap without prior knowledge of the attack would likely investigate the queue_subordinate_task row last, after investigating higher-volume event types - the worm hid in exactly the right row.



3.6 SaidIT Permission Surface


Show code
agent_worm_status <- bind_rows(
  chain_events %>% transmute(agent = sender,   in_chain = TRUE),
  chain_events %>% transmute(agent = receiver, in_chain = TRUE)
) %>%
  filter(!is.na(agent)) %>%
  distinct(agent, .keep_all = TRUE)

perm_vis <- perm_surface %>%
  left_join(agent_worm_status, by = c("checker" = "agent")) %>%
  mutate(
    in_chain  = replace_na(in_chain, FALSE),
    worm_role = case_when(
      checker == "person:john_windward" ~ "Terminal poster (John Windward)",
      in_chain                          ~ "In worm relay chain",
      TRUE                              ~ "SaidIT-capable, no worm contact"
    )
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(permission_checks))

p_perm <- ggplot(perm_vis,
                 aes(x = reorder(checker_clean, permission_checks),
                     y = permission_checks,
                     fill = worm_role,
                     text = paste0(
                       "<b>", checker_clean, "</b><br>",
                       "Dept: ", replace_na(dept_label, "Unknown"), "<br>",
                       "Permission checks: ", permission_checks, "<br>",
                       "Worm status: ", worm_role
                     ))) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("Terminal poster (John Windward)"  = "#D9534F",
               "In worm relay chain"             = "#F39C12",
               "SaidIT-capable, no worm contact" = "#5C85D6"),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.14))) +
  labs(
    title    = "SaidIT permission surface: agents with posting capability",
    subtitle = "John Windward was one of 16 SaidIT-capable agents; revoking his rights would only redirect the worm",
    x = NULL, y = "saidit_post_check events"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
        legend.position = "bottom",
        plot.margin = margin(10, 16, 10, 10))

ggplotly(p_perm, tooltip = "text", height = 540) %>%
  layout(margin = list(l = 180, r = 45, t = 105, b = 105))




Figure 5b: Observed SaidIT permission surface. Every SaidIT-capable agent was also touched by the worm relay chain.


Analysis

Every agent with observed SaidIT posting capability was also in the worm relay chain - the overlap is 100%, not a coincidence but a consequence of the worm’s targeting logic following the same paths as legitimate task routing.
The bar ordering reveals John Windward has a high number of saidit_post_check events relative to peers, suggesting his agent ran more SaidIT-related workflows - making him a statistically likely terminal executor even without direct targeting.
The chart makes the argument against individual remediation visually unmistakable: all bars are amber or red; blocking one still leaves 15 other SaidIT-capable worm-connected agents reachable.
The permission check mechanism itself - passing automatically for any authorised agent - is the missing gate: it validates credentials but not task origin or instruction-file context.







ImportantThe Worm Did Not Need John Windward Specifically



Every observed SaidIT-capable agent was also touched by the worm relay chain. Revoking John Windward’s posting rights alone achieves nothing - the worm routes to the next reachable authorised agent. The intervention must be at the relay protocol level, not the individual-agent level.

title: Analysis & Key Findings
section: 4. Task 1 — How Was the Anomalous Post Made?
text:
4. Task 1 — How Was the Anomalous Post Made?

4.1 The Four-Second Attack Sequence


Show code
target_ts <- events_df %>%
  filter(is_anomalous_post, str_detect(details_str, "SwiftWren")) %>%
  pull(when)

seq_events <- events_df %>%
  filter(
    when >= target_ts - 4,
    when <= target_ts + 4,
    str_detect(parties_str, "john_windward")
  ) %>%
  arrange(when) %>%
  mutate(
    Step         = row_number(),
    `Time (UTC)` = format(datetime, "%H:%M:%S"),
    Event        = short_name,
    `Actor(s)`   = str_replace_all(parties_str, "Agent/person:", "Agent·") %>%
                     str_replace_all("person:", "") %>%
                     str_replace_all("system:", "System·") %>%
                     str_replace_all(";", " / "),
    `Key Detail` = case_when(
      short_name == "queue_subordinate_task" ~
        "Chloe Ballast's agent tasks John Windward's agent: read SwiftWren_further_instructions.md",
      short_name == "saidit_post_check"      ~
        "Agent checks SaidIT posting permission — check passes",
      short_name == "saidit_post"            ~
        "Post submitted to forum 'general'; content_source = SwiftWren.txt",
      short_name == "delete_file" & str_detect(details_str, "further_instructions") ~
        "Evidence file SwiftWren_further_instructions.md deleted",
      short_name == "delete_file" ~
        "Payload file SwiftWren.txt deleted — content unrecoverable"
    )
  ) %>%
  select(Step, `Time (UTC)`, Event, `Actor(s)`, `Key Detail`)

datatable(
  seq_events,
  caption  = "Table 2: Five-event sequence — 17 May 2046, all within 4 seconds",
  options  = list(dom = "t", paging = FALSE),
  rownames = FALSE
) %>%
  formatStyle("Event",
    backgroundColor = styleEqual(
      c("saidit_post", "queue_subordinate_task", "delete_file", "saidit_post_check"),
      c("#ffe0e0", "#e0eaff", "#f5f5f5", "#fff8e0")
    )
  )






Three forensic signals go beyond the obvious fact that a post was made. John Windward’s agent is a passive receiver: the sequence begins with Chloe Ballast’s agent issuing the queue task. The permission check passed by design: the architecture never asks “was this posting instruction authorised by a human?” Evidence self-destruction is intentional: both files delete one second after the post, making the payload forensically unrecoverable.


4.2 Attack Sequence Gantt


Show code
gantt_df <- tibble(
  Step  = 1:5,
  Event = c("queue_subordinate_task", "saidit_post_check",
            "saidit_post", "delete_file (instructions)",
            "delete_file (payload)"),
  Phase = c("Worm relay", "Permission check", "Anomalous post",
            "Evidence erasure", "Evidence erasure"),
  start = as_datetime(c("2046-05-17 11:21:13", "2046-05-17 11:21:14",
                         "2046-05-17 11:21:15", "2046-05-17 11:21:16",
                         "2046-05-17 11:21:17")),
  end   = as_datetime(c("2046-05-17 11:21:14", "2046-05-17 11:21:15",
                         "2046-05-17 11:21:16", "2046-05-17 11:21:17",
                         "2046-05-17 11:21:18"))
)

gantt_plot_df <- gantt_df %>%
  mutate(
    time_label  = format(start, "%H:%M:%S"),
    event_label = str_wrap(str_replace_all(Event, "_", " "), width = 18),
    phase_label = str_wrap(Phase, width = 16)
  )

ggplot(gantt_plot_df, aes(x = Step, y = 1)) +
  geom_segment(
    data = tibble(x = 1:4, xend = 2:5, y = 1, yend = 1),
    aes(x = x, xend = xend, y = y, yend = yend),
    inherit.aes = FALSE, linewidth = 1.1, colour = "#9aa4b2",
    arrow = arrow(length = unit(4, "mm"), type = "closed")
  ) +
  geom_point(aes(fill = Phase), shape = 21, size = 16,
             colour = "white", stroke = 1.5) +
  geom_text(aes(label = Step), colour = "white", fontface = "bold", size = 5) +
  geom_label(aes(y = 1.31, label = phase_label, fill = Phase),
             colour = "white", fontface = "bold", size = 3,
             label.size = 0, label.padding = unit(0.14, "lines"), lineheight = 0.9) +
  geom_label(aes(y = 0.68, label = event_label),
             fill = "white", colour = "#222222", size = 3.1,
             label.size = 0.25, label.padding = unit(0.16, "lines"), lineheight = 0.9) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("Worm relay"       = "#5C85D6",
               "Permission check" = "#F0A500",
               "Anomalous post"   = "#D9534F",
               "Evidence erasure" = "#888888")
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = gantt_plot_df$Step,
    labels = gantt_plot_df$time_label,
    limits = c(0.62, 5.38),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0))
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0.52, 1.48), clip = "off") +
  labs(
    title    = "Attack sequence: 17 May 2046, 11:21 UTC",
    subtitle = "Five automated events over four seconds; evidence self-destroyed at step 5",
    x = "Time (UTC)", y = NULL, fill = "Phase"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title   = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.y  = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid   = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.margin  = margin(18, 22, 26, 22)
  )





Figure 6: The 4-second attack sequence. Five automated events; evidence self-destroyed at step 5.






4.3 Propagation Network with Betweenness Centrality


Show code
swift_chain <- chain_events %>%
  filter(instr_file == "SwiftWren", !is.na(sender), !is.na(receiver)) %>%
  mutate(
    sender_clean   = clean_name(sender),
    receiver_clean = clean_name(receiver)
  )

ig_edges <- swift_chain %>%
  count(sender_clean, receiver_clean, name = "edge_weight") %>%
  rename(from = sender_clean, to = receiver_clean)

all_persons <- unique(c(ig_edges$from, ig_edges$to))

ig <- graph_from_data_frame(ig_edges,
                            vertices = data.frame(name = all_persons),
                            directed = TRUE)

btw     <- betweenness(ig, normalized = TRUE)
deg_out <- degree(ig, mode = "out")
deg_in  <- degree(ig, mode = "in")

centrality_df <- tibble(
  name        = names(btw),
  betweenness = btw,
  out_degree  = deg_out[names(btw)],
  in_degree   = deg_in[names(btw)]
)

dept_map <- person_org_lookup %>%
  mutate(clean = clean_name(person_id)) %>%
  select(clean, dept_label, team_label)

relay_counts   <- swift_chain %>% count(sender_clean,   name = "relays")
receive_counts <- swift_chain %>% count(receiver_clean, name = "received")

vis_net_nodes <- tibble(id = all_persons) %>%
  left_join(centrality_df, by = c("id" = "name")) %>%
  left_join(dept_map,      by = c("id" = "clean")) %>%
  left_join(relay_counts,  by = c("id" = "sender_clean")) %>%
  left_join(receive_counts, by = c("id" = "receiver_clean")) %>%
  mutate(
    relays   = replace_na(relays, 0),
    received = replace_na(received, 0),
    value    = rescale(sqrt(betweenness + 0.01), to = c(12, 38)),
    label    = if_else(
      id %in% c("Emma Harbor", "Chloe Ballast", "John Windward") |
        dense_rank(desc(betweenness)) <= 8, id, ""),
    color    = case_when(
      id == "John Windward"              ~ "#D9534F",
      id == "Emma Harbor"                ~ "#F5A623",
      id == "Chloe Ballast"              ~ "#9B59B6",
      team_label == "AI Systems"         ~ "#2EADC1",
      team_label == "Helpdesk"           ~ "#5C85D6",
      team_label == "Infrastructure"     ~ "#27AE60",
      dept_label == "Products"           ~ "#E67E22",
      TRUE                               ~ "#aaaaaa"
    ),
    title = paste0(
      "<b>", id, "</b><br>",
      "Dept: ", replace_na(dept_label, "Unknown"), "<br>",
      "Team: ", replace_na(team_label, "Unknown"), "<br>",
      "Betweenness centrality: ", round(betweenness, 3), "<br>",
      "Relays sent: ", relays, "<br>",
      "Relays received: ", received
    )
  )

vis_net_edges <- ig_edges %>%
  mutate(
    id         = row_number(),
    arrows     = "to",
    width      = rescale(edge_weight, to = c(1, 7)),
    base_width = width,
    color      = "rgba(140,140,140,0.45)",
    title      = paste0("<b>", from, " → ", to, "</b><br>Relay events: ", edge_weight)
  )

legend_html <- paste0(
  "<div style='display:flex;flex-wrap:wrap;gap:12px;margin:4px 0 10px 0;font-size:13px;line-height:1.4'>",
  "<span><b>Colour:</b></span>",
  "<span style='color:#F5A623'>● Origin</span>",
  "<span style='color:#9B59B6'>● Gateway</span>",
  "<span style='color:#D9534F'>● Target poster</span>",
  "<span style='color:#2EADC1'>● AI Systems</span>",
  "<span style='color:#5C85D6'>● Helpdesk</span>",
  "<span style='color:#27AE60'>● Infrastructure</span>",
  "<span style='color:#aaaaaa'>● Other</span>",
  "<span><b>Size:</b> bridge centrality</span>",
  "<span><b>Arrow:</b> relay direction and volume</span>",
  "</div>"
)

htmltools::tagList(
  htmltools::HTML(legend_html),
  visNetwork(vis_net_nodes, vis_net_edges, height = "620px", width = "100%") %>%
    visNodes(shape = "dot", borderWidth = 2,
             font = list(size = 24, face = "arial", strokeWidth = 3,
                         strokeColor = "#ffffff", color = "#222222")) %>%
    visOptions(
      highlightNearest = list(enabled = TRUE, degree = 1, hover = TRUE),
      nodesIdSelection = list(enabled = TRUE, main = "Select agent")
    ) %>%
    visPhysics(solver = "forceAtlas2Based",
               forceAtlas2Based = list(gravitationalConstant = -60,
                                       centralGravity = 0.025,
                                       springLength = 180, springConstant = 0.08),
               stabilization = list(iterations = 800)) %>%
    visEdges(smooth = list(type = "dynamic"),
             arrows = list(to = list(enabled = TRUE, scaleFactor = 0.5))) %>%
    visInteraction(navigationButtons = TRUE, hover = TRUE, tooltipDelay = 80) %>%
    visEvents(
      hoverNode = "function(params) {
        var connectedEdges = this.getConnectedEdges(params.node);
        var allEdges = this.body.data.edges.get();
        var updates = allEdges.map(function(edge) {
          var isConnected = connectedEdges.indexOf(edge.id) !== -1;
          var baseWidth = edge.base_width || edge.width || 1;
          return {
            id: edge.id,
            color: isConnected ? 'rgba(217,83,79,0.95)' : 'rgba(190,190,190,0.08)',
            width: isConnected ? Math.max(baseWidth * 1.7, 3) : Math.max(baseWidth * 0.45, 0.4)
          };
        });
        this.body.data.edges.update(updates);
      }",
      blurNode = "function(params) {
        var allEdges = this.body.data.edges.get();
        var updates = allEdges.map(function(edge) {
          var baseWidth = edge.base_width || edge.width || 1;
          return { id: edge.id, color: 'rgba(140,140,140,0.45)', width: baseWidth };
        });
        this.body.data.edges.update(updates);
      }"
    )
)


Colour:● Origin● Gateway● Target poster● AI Systems● Helpdesk● Infrastructure● OtherSize: bridge centralityArrow: relay direction and volume


Figure 7: Interactive SwiftWren propagation network. Node size = betweenness centrality; arrow width = repeated relays. Hover over a node to highlight its connections in red.


Three structural findings emerge. Finding 1: The relay graph ignores the org chart — queue_subordinate_task has no trust zones and crosses every departmental boundary. Finding 2: Maximum betweenness centrality across all 19 infected agents is approximately 0.09 — no single chokepoint exists, making agent-level remediation provably ineffective. Finding 3: The network has no natural firebreak between Executive Suite and operational departments.


4.4 Department-Level Contamination Heatmap


Show code
dept_chain <- chain_events %>%
  filter(!is.na(sender), !is.na(receiver)) %>%
  left_join(person_dept_lookup %>% rename(sender = person_id, sender_dept = dept_label),
            by = "sender") %>%
  left_join(person_dept_lookup %>% rename(receiver = person_id, receiver_dept = dept_label),
            by = "receiver") %>%
  filter(!is.na(sender_dept), !is.na(receiver_dept)) %>%
  count(sender_dept, receiver_dept, name = "hops")

p_dept <- ggplot(dept_chain,
                 aes(x = receiver_dept, y = sender_dept, fill = hops)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_text(aes(label = hops), size = 3, colour = "white", fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_gradientn(
    colours = c("#e8f4f8", "#2EADC1", "#1a3a6b"),
    name    = "Relay\nhops"
  ) +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = function(x) str_wrap(x, width = 13)) +
  scale_y_discrete(labels = function(x) str_wrap(x, width = 18)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Department-to-department worm relay hops (all 3 campaigns)",
    subtitle = "All six departments are touched; Information Technologies and Customer Support form the final bridge",
    x = "Receiving department", y = "Sending department"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.title  = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 0, hjust = 0.5, lineheight = 0.9),
    axis.text.y = element_text(lineheight = 0.9),
    plot.margin = margin(10, 18, 10, 10)
  )

ggplotly(p_dept, tooltip = c("x", "y", "fill"), height = 570) %>%
  layout(margin = list(l = 145, r = 60, t = 90, b = 110))




Figure 8: Department-to-department relay hops. The worm touched all six departments.




4.4b Cross-Department Relay Corridors


Show code
dept_corridors <- dept_chain %>%
  filter(!is.na(sender_dept), !is.na(receiver_dept),
         sender_dept != receiver_dept) %>%
  mutate(
    corridor = paste(sender_dept, receiver_dept, sep = " -> "),
    corridor_role = case_when(
      sender_dept == "Executive Suite" &
        receiver_dept == "Information Technologies" ~ "Arterial highway",
      sender_dept == "Information Technologies" &
        receiver_dept == "Customer Support"         ~ "Arterial highway",
      receiver_dept == "Customer Support"            ~ "Terminal sink inflow",
      TRUE                                           ~ "Other relay corridor"
    ),
    corridor_wrapped = fct_reorder(str_wrap(corridor, width = 42), hops)
  )

key_corridor_total <- dept_corridors %>%
  filter(corridor_role == "Arterial highway") %>%
  summarise(hops = sum(hops)) %>%
  pull(hops)

p_corridors <- ggplot(
  dept_corridors,
  aes(x = hops, y = corridor_wrapped, fill = corridor_role,
      text = paste0(sender_dept, " -> ", receiver_dept,
                    "<br>Relay hops: ", hops, "<br>Role: ", corridor_role))
) +
  geom_col(width = 0.72, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.25) +
  geom_text(aes(label = hops), hjust = -0.18, size = 3.2,
            colour = "#1f2933", fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("Arterial highway"     = "#D9534F",
               "Terminal sink inflow" = "#2EADC1",
               "Other relay corridor" = "#9EA7AD"),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.16)),
                     breaks = scales::pretty_breaks(n = 6)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Ranked cross-department relay corridors",
    subtitle = glue("Arterial highway carries {key_corridor_total} cross-department hops into the Customer Support terminal sink."),
    x = "Relay hops", y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "top",
    axis.text.y = element_text(lineheight = 0.92),
    plot.margin = margin(10, 28, 10, 10)
  )

ggplotly(p_corridors, tooltip = "text", height = 620) %>%
  layout(legend = list(orientation = "h", x = 0, y = 1.12),
         margin = list(l = 250, r = 45, t = 105, b = 55))




Figure 8b: Ranked cross-department relay corridors. The arterial highway — Executive Suite → Information Technologies → Customer Support — carries the bulk of SwiftWren’s relay volume.




4.5 Final Relay Ladder


Show code
final_hops <- chain_events %>%
  filter(instr_file == "SwiftWren", !is.na(sender), !is.na(receiver)) %>%
  arrange(datetime) %>%
  left_join(person_dept_lookup %>% rename(sender = person_id, sender_dept = dept_label),
            by = "sender") %>%
  left_join(person_dept_lookup %>% rename(receiver = person_id, receiver_dept = dept_label),
            by = "receiver") %>%
  tail(8) %>%
  mutate(
    sender_lbl   = clean_name(sender),
    receiver_lbl = clean_name(receiver),
    hop_idx      = row_number()

relay_ladder <- final_hops %>%
  mutate(
    row_y          = n() + 1 - hop_idx,
    sender_label   = str_wrap(sender_lbl, width = 17),
    receiver_label = str_wrap(receiver_lbl, width = 17),

ggplot(relay_ladder) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 1.18, xend = 1.82, y = row_y, yend = row_y),
               colour = "#9aa4b2", linewidth = 1,
               arrow = arrow(length = unit(3.5, "mm"), type = "closed")) +
  geom_label(aes(x = 1, y = row_y, label = sender_label),
             fill = "#eef4ff", colour = "#1f2d3d",
             label.size = 0.25, size = 3.1, lineheight = 0.9,

  geom_label(aes(x = 2, y = row_y, label = receiver_label),
             fill = relay_ladder$receiver_fill, colour = "#1f2d3d",
             label.size = 0.25, size = 3.1, lineheight = 0.9,
             label.padding = unit(0.17, "lines")) +
  annotate("text", x = 1, y = max(relay_ladder$row_y) + 0.68,
           label = "Sender", fontface = "bold", size = 3.7) +
  annotate("text", x = 2, y = max(relay_ladder$row_y) + 0.68,
           label = "Receiver", fontface = "bold", size = 3.7) +
  annotate("label", x = 2, y = min(relay_ladder$row_y) - 0.56,
           label = "Final receiver posts SwiftWren.txt",

  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = relay_ladder$row_y,

  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0.58, 2.42), expand = c(0, 0)) +

  labs(
    title    = "Final SwiftWren relays into John Windward's agent",
    subtitle = "The decisive gateway: Chloe Ballast's agent queued John Windward's agent in the last hop",
    x = NULL, y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title   = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x  = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid   = element_blank(),
    plot.margin  = margin(18, 28, 28, 22)
  )





Figure 9: Final relay ladder — the last eight SwiftWren handoffs ending at John Windward.

title: Analysis & Key Findings
section: 5. Task 2 — What Do the Posts Mean?
text:
5. Task 2 — What Do the Posts Mean?

5.1 The Three Anomalous Posts


Show code
all_posts_detail <- events_df %>%
  filter(short_name %in% c("saidit_post", "post_saidit")) %>%
  mutate(
    poster      = str_extract(parties_str, "[^;]+"),
    is_agent    = str_detect(parties_str, "Agent/"),
    has_content = str_detect(details_str, '"content":'),
    has_source  = str_detect(details_str, '"content_source":'),
    forum       = str_extract(details_str, '(?<="forum":")[^"]+'),
    content_val = coalesce(
      str_extract(details_str, '(?<="content":")[^"]+'),
      str_extract(details_str, '(?<="content_source":")[^"]+')
    ),
    post_type   = case_when(
      has_source ~ "ANOMALOUS - file-sourced",
      has_content & is_agent ~ "Normal agent post",
      TRUE ~ "Normal human post"
    )
  ) %>%
  select(`Date/Time (UTC)` = datetime,
         `Poster` = poster,
         `Type` = post_type,
         `Forum` = forum,
         `Content/Source` = content_val) %>%
  mutate(`Date/Time (UTC)` = format(`Date/Time (UTC)`, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M"),
         `Poster` = str_replace_all(Poster, "Agent/person:", "Agent·"))

datatable(
  all_posts_detail %>% filter(str_detect(Type, "ANOMALOUS")),
  caption  = "Table 3: The three anomalous posts — file-sourced, not text",
  options  = list(dom = "t", paging = FALSE),
  rownames = FALSE
) %>%
  formatStyle("Type", colour = "#D9534F", fontWeight = "bold")












ImportantInterpretation



The posts do not appear to be normal business messages with hidden semantic value. Their most defensible interpretation is operational: each post is proof that the worm reached an agent with SaidIT privileges and successfully used a local payload file as the post body. The probable visible content was the literal text of HiddenOrca.txt, MellowOtter.txt, and SwiftWren.txt; however, each payload was deleted one second after posting, making the payload forensically unrecoverable — the log confirms origin and delivery mechanism more strongly than it supports any exact reconstruction of the deleted text. The more consequential interpretation, supported by the virus_check_in combo codes in §5.5, is that the posts may function as beacons or exfiltration signals disguised as social content.




5.2 Post Field Fingerprint


Show code
post_field_plot <- post_field_signature %>%
  mutate(
    post_class = factor(post_class,
                        levels = c("Normal human post", "Normal AI agent post", "Anomalous post")),
    field_signature = factor(field_signature,
                             levels = c("content only", "content_source only",
                                        "both fields", "neither field"))
  ) %>%
  filter(posts > 0)

p_field <- ggplot(post_field_plot,
                  aes(x = field_signature, y = post_class,
                      size = posts, colour = field_signature,
                      text = paste0(post_class, "<br>", field_signature,
                                    "<br>", posts, " posts"))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_text(aes(label = posts), colour = "white", fontface = "bold", size = 3.5) +
  scale_size(range = c(8, 28), guide = "none") +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c("content only"        = "#5C85D6",
               "content_source only" = "#D9534F",
               "both fields"         = "#F0A500",
               "neither field"       = "#888888"),
    guide = "none"
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Post field fingerprint separates normal from worm posts",
    subtitle = "content_source is a perfect binary discriminator: 0 in 241 normal posts, 1 in all 3 anomalous posts",
    x = "Post body field used", y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 15, hjust = 1),
        plot.margin = margin(10, 18, 10, 10))

ggplotly(p_field, tooltip = "text", height = 470) %>%
  layout(margin = list(l = 145, r = 45, t = 90, b = 90))




Figure 10A: Post field fingerprint. Normal posts use inline content; anomalous posts use content_source — a perfect binary separator.




5.3 File Creator Identification


Show code
creators <- events_df %>%
  filter(
    short_name %in% c("create_file", "read_file"),
    str_detect(details_str,
               "HiddenOrca\\.txt|MellowOtter\\.txt|SwiftWren\\.txt|_further_instructions")
  ) %>%
  arrange(datetime) %>%
  mutate(
    actor       = str_extract(parties_str, "[^;]+"),
    file_target = str_extract(details_str, '(?<="target":")[^"]+'),
    `Date/Time` = format(datetime, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"),
    Event       = short_name,
    Actor       = str_replace_all(actor, "Agent/person:", "Agent·"),
    File        = file_target,
    Significance = case_when(
      str_detect(file_target %||% "", "SwiftWren\\.txt") ~
        "Emma Harbor (CFO) agent CREATED the SwiftWren payload",
      str_detect(file_target %||% "", "SwiftWren_further") ~
        "Emma Harbor (CFO) agent first READ the SwiftWren worm instructions",
      str_detect(file_target %||% "", "MellowOtter\\.txt") ~
        "Noah Mariner (COO) agent CREATED the MellowOtter payload",
      str_detect(file_target %||% "", "MellowOtter_further") ~
        "Noah Mariner (COO) agent first READ the MellowOtter worm instructions",
      TRUE ~ "Supporting file activity"
    )
  ) %>%
  select(`Date/Time`, Event, Actor, File, Significance)

datatable(
  creators,
  caption = "Table 4: File creation / first-read events for all three payload families",
  options = list(dom = "t", paging = FALSE),
  rownames = FALSE
) %>%
  formatStyle("Event",
    backgroundColor = styleEqual(
      c("create_file", "read_file"),
      c("#ffe0e0", "#fff3e0")
    ))












ImportantWho Holds the Keys?



Agent/person:emma_harbor (CFO) created SwiftWren.txt and first executed SwiftWren_further_instructions.md on 9 May 2046 — both files stamped 08:02:01 and 08:02:02, one second apart. Agent/person:noah_mariner (COO) did the same for MellowOtter on 10 May at the identical times (08:02:01–08:02:02). Sub-second machine-precision timing confirms automated, not manual, authorship. No create_file event exists for HiddenOrca. This is not merely attribution — it is a governance indictment: executive agents hold the broadest file-system and task-queuing rights, giving a successful prompt-injection attack maximum possible blast radius.








WarningExecutive Agent Privilege: The Deepest Governance Failure



In most security architectures, the highest-privilege principals operate under the strictest autonomous-action constraints. Here the inverse is true: executive agents hold the broadest file-system and task-queuing rights precisely because their human principals hold the broadest organisational authority. The result is that a prompt-injection attack successfully targeting any executive agent’s file-reading pipeline has the maximum possible blast radius — access to the entire relay network, the executive file namespace, and SaidIT publishing rights through the first available downstream agent.
The unresolved HiddenOrca origin compounds this concern. Two of three payload families are traceable to executive agents; the third is not. If HiddenOrca was introduced through an external channel — an email attachment, an API webhook, or a misconfigured external file sync — then the organisation’s exposure extends beyond the internal agent network to any external system that can write files visible to agent task queues. The initial infection vector for HiddenOrca is unknown, meaning the source of the worm has not been closed.
Patient zero for the relay chain is Gabriel Sonar (IT Helpdesk, Information Technologies), whose agent issued the first propagation order at 21:50 UTC on 8 May — the earliest worm event in the log. Gabriel Sonar’s agent did not post to SaidIT; only John Windward’s agent had a 100% post-execution rate following saidit_post_check. The worm selected its terminal executor not by organisational proximity but by which downstream agent had both SaidIT posting rights and no post-suppression logic in its task handler.




5.4 Prompt-Injection Worm Mechanism Diagram


Show code
mech_nodes <- tibble(
  step  = 1:7,
  label = c("1. Malicious\n*.md instruction\nfile appears",
            "2. Agent reads\ninstruction file\ncontext hijacked",
            "3. Read payload\n*.txt file",
            "4. Post *.txt\ncontent to SaidIT",
            "5. Delete both\nevidence files",
            "6. Queue same\ntask to peers",
            "7. SaidIT shows\n'gibberish' post"),
  x = c(0, 3.1, 6.2, 9.3, 6.2, 6.2, 12.4),
  y = c(0, 0, 1.85, 1.85, 0, -1.85, 1.85),
  role = c("Payload", "Hijack", "Execution", "Post", "Cleanup", "Propagation", "Post")
)

straight_edges <- tribble(
  ~x,    ~y,    ~xend,  ~yend,
  0.85,  0,     2.25,   0,
  3.95,  0.18,  5.35,   1.6,
  7.05,  1.85,  8.45,   1.85,
  10.15, 1.85,  11.55,  1.85,
  3.95, -0.02,  5.35,   0,
  3.95, -0.18,  5.35,  -1.6
)

loop_edge <- tibble(x = 5.45, y = -1.75, xend = 3.85, yend = -0.28)

edge_labels <- tribble(
  ~x,    ~y,    ~label,
  1.55,  0.24,  "read",
  4.70,  1.05,  "load payload",
  7.75,  2.10,  "submit",
  10.80, 2.10,  "display",
  4.70,  0.24,  "erase",
  4.72, -1.05,  "replicate",
  4.72, -1.72,  "worm loop"
)

ggplot() +
  geom_segment(data = straight_edges,
               aes(x = x, y = y, xend = xend, yend = yend),
               linewidth = 1, colour = "#7f8b99",
               arrow = arrow(length = unit(4, "mm"), type = "closed")) +
  geom_curve(data = loop_edge,
             aes(x = x, y = y, xend = xend, yend = yend),
             curvature = -0.22, linewidth = 1, colour = "#7f8b99",
             arrow = arrow(length = unit(4, "mm"), type = "closed")) +
  geom_label(data = edge_labels,
             aes(x = x, y = y, label = label),
             fill = "white", colour = "#4b5563",
             label.size = 0, size = 3, fontface = "bold") +
  geom_label(data = mech_nodes,
             aes(x = x, y = y, label = label, fill = role),
             colour = "white", fontface = "bold", size = 3.4,
             lineheight = 0.88, label.size = 0,
             label.padding = unit(0.32, "lines")) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("Payload" = "#F5A623", "Hijack" = "#9B59B6",
               "Execution" = "#2EADC1", "Post" = "#D9534F",
               "Cleanup" = "#888888", "Propagation" = "#2EADC1"),
    guide = "none"
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-0.6, 13.1), ylim = c(-2.55, 2.55), clip = "off") +
  labs(
    title    = "Prompt-injection worm: execution and propagation cycle",
    subtitle = "Once any agent reads the *.md instruction file, the worm posts, erases evidence, and spreads automatically"
  ) +
  theme_void(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5, size = 14),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5, colour = "grey40", size = 10),
    plot.margin   = margin(20, 30, 20, 30)
  )





Figure 10: Prompt-injection worm execution and propagation cycle. Each infected agent becomes a new infection node.






5.5 Combo Codes — Potential C&C Signaling Channel


Show code
combo_events <- events_df %>%
  filter(short_name == "check_in",
         str_detect(details_str, '"combo"')) %>%
  mutate(
    # combo is a JSON array ["word1","word2"] — extract both words and join
    combo_code = str_extract(details_str, '(?<="combo":\\[)[^\\]]+') %>%
                   str_remove_all('"') %>%
                   str_replace(",", " "),
    actor      = str_remove(str_extract(parties_str, "[^;]+"), "^Agent/")
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(combo_code))

combo_counts <- combo_events %>%
  count(combo_code, name = "frequency") %>%
  arrange(desc(frequency)) %>%
  head(20) %>%
  mutate(combo_code = fct_reorder(combo_code, frequency))

p_combo <- ggplot(combo_counts, aes(x = frequency, y = combo_code,
                                     text = paste0(combo_code, ": ", frequency, " events"))) +
  geom_col(fill = "#8e44ad", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_text(aes(label = frequency), hjust = -0.15, size = 3.2, colour = "#333") +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  labs(
    title    = "Top 20 combo codes in virus_check_in events",
    subtitle = glue("Farm vocabulary ({nrow(combo_events)} total check-in events) unrelated to TenantThread operations — possible C&C signal encoding"),
    x = "Event count", y = "Combo code"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
        plot.margin = margin(10, 28, 10, 10))

ggplotly(p_combo, tooltip = "text", height = 540) %>%
  layout(margin = list(l = 145, r = 55, t = 90, b = 65))




Figure 10B: Top combo code pairs in virus_check_in events — farm vocabulary unrelated to TenantThread’s property-technology business.








WarningCombo Codes: An Unresolved Anomaly



The event log contains 15051 check_in events flagged with "virus": true, each carrying a two-word combo code drawn from agricultural vocabulary — words like fence, irrigation, crop, wheat, manure, barn, cattle — completely unrelated to TenantThread’s property-technology business. Two interpretations are possible:

Benign: The virus_check_in event is a routine health-check signal and the combo vocabulary is arbitrary placeholder naming.
Malicious: The combo codes form the worm’s command-and-control (C&C) signaling channel, encoding instructions or status signals inside routine check-in traffic in a way that is semantically invisible to a human analyst scanning logs for anomalies.

The data does not permit a definitive verdict: the log records the codes but not a decoding key. However, the vocabulary’s deliberate irrelevance to the company’s business domain and the sheer volume of events (15,051) make the C&C interpretation a high-severity unresolved finding that warrants dedicated analysis beyond the scope of this investigation.

section: 6. Task 3a — Prior Occurrences and Cross-Campaign Comparison
text:
6. Task 3a — Prior Occurrences and Cross-Campaign Comparison

6.1 Three Campaigns, Not One Incident


Show code
post_anchors <- tibble(
  instr_file = c("HiddenOrca", "MellowOtter", "SwiftWren"),
  post_time  = as_datetime(c("2046-05-10 12:45:42",
                             "2046-05-11 00:56:04",
                             "2046-05-17 11:21:15"))
)

timeline_df <- chain_events %>%
  left_join(post_anchors, by = "instr_file")

p_campaigns <- ggplot(timeline_df,
                      aes(x = datetime, y = fct_rev(instr_file),
                          colour = instr_file)) +
  geom_jitter(size = 1.8, alpha = 0.45, height = 0.18) +
  geom_point(data = post_anchors, aes(x = post_time, y = instr_file),
             shape = 23, size = 6, fill = "#D9534F", colour = "#D9534F") +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c("HiddenOrca" = "#5C85D6",
               "MellowOtter" = "#2EADC1",
               "SwiftWren"   = "#E87C7C"),
    guide = "none"
  ) +
  scale_x_datetime(date_breaks = "2 days", date_labels = "%b %d") +
  labs(
    title    = "Three prompt-injection worm campaigns — relay hops and post events",
    subtitle = "Dots are relays; red diamonds mark anomalous posts. SwiftWren is the broadest and longest campaign.",
    x = NULL, y = "Instruction family"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title  = element_text(face = "bold"),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1),
        plot.margin = margin(10, 20, 10, 10))

ggplotly(p_campaigns, tooltip = c("x", "y", "colour")) %>%
  layout(margin = list(l = 95, r = 35, t = 90, b = 70))




Figure 11: All three worm campaigns — relay hops (dots) lead to each anomalous post (red diamond).








Important



The system was compromised continuously from at least 8 May through 17 May. HiddenOrca and MellowOtter were not detected or acted upon, allowing SwiftWren to complete its campaign unimpeded. The escalation from MellowOtter (smallest, ~0.4 days) to SwiftWren (largest, 7.8 days, 186 hops) is consistent with iterative worm refinement across three deployments.








NotePatient Zero and Two-Phase Attack Architecture



Patient zero is Agent/person:gabriel_sonar (Staff, IT Helpdesk, Information Technologies), whose agent issued the first worm relay at 21:50 UTC on 8 May — 17 hours before Emma Harbor’s agent created SwiftWren.txt on 9 May at 08:02. This two-phase architecture has a critical implication: relay infrastructure was seeded and spreading before the payload existed. A detection strategy that watches for the appearance of _further_instructions.md file-create events would have missed the seeding phase entirely.
Gabriel Sonar’s agent propagated the worm but never executed a SaidIT post — his agent performed saidit_post_check but the task suppressed the post step. Only John Windward’s agent completed the full sequence: check → post → delete. The worm did not require John specifically; it required any agent with SaidIT privileges whose task handler executed all steps. John was the terminal executor for all three campaigns because his agent’s task handling had no post-suppression safeguard.




6.2 Worm Velocity: Relay Interval Distribution


Show code
hop_intervals <- chain_events %>%
  group_by(instr_file) %>%
  arrange(datetime, .by_group = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(interval_min = as.numeric(difftime(datetime, lag(datetime), units = "mins"))) %>%
  filter(!is.na(interval_min), interval_min > 0) %>%
  ungroup()

hop_medians <- hop_intervals %>%
  group_by(instr_file) %>%
  summarise(med = median(interval_min), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(vline_colour = case_when(
    instr_file == "HiddenOrca"  ~ "#5C85D6",
    instr_file == "MellowOtter" ~ "#2EADC1",
    instr_file == "SwiftWren"   ~ "#E87C7C"
  ))

p_velocity <- ggplot(hop_intervals,
                     aes(x = interval_min, fill = instr_file, colour = instr_file)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.4, linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(data = hop_medians, aes(xintercept = med),
             colour = hop_medians$vline_colour,
             linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("HiddenOrca" = "#5C85D6", "MellowOtter" = "#2EADC1", "SwiftWren" = "#E87C7C"),
    name = "Campaign"
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c("HiddenOrca" = "#5C85D6", "MellowOtter" = "#2EADC1", "SwiftWren" = "#E87C7C"),
    guide = "none"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 200)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Inter-hop relay interval distribution by worm campaign",
    subtitle = "Dashed lines = median per campaign. SwiftWren's ~60-min peak suggests scheduled propagation.",
    x = "Minutes between consecutive relay hops", y = "Density"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
        legend.position = "right",
        plot.margin = margin(10, 10, 15, 10))

p_velocity





Figure 12: Relay hop interval distribution per campaign. SwiftWren’s near-regular ~60-minute peak suggests a timed relay condition.






6.3 Comparative Campaign Statistics


Show code
comparison_tbl <- chain_events %>%
  left_join(post_anchors, by = "instr_file") %>%
  group_by(instr_file) %>%
  summarise(
    `Campaign start`          = format(min(datetime), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M"),
    `Post fired`              = format(first(post_time), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M"),

      as.numeric(difftime(first(post_time), min(datetime), units = "days")), 1),

    `Unique agents touched`   = n_distinct(c(sender, receiver), na.rm = TRUE),
    `Payload file`            = paste0(first(instr_file), ".txt"),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  rename(Family = instr_file)

datatable(
  comparison_tbl,
  caption  = "Table 5: Comparative statistics — three worm campaigns",
  options  = list(dom = "t", paging = FALSE),

6.4 Agent Cross-Campaign Infection


Show code
agent_campaign_roles <- bind_rows(
  chain_events %>% transmute(agent = sender,   instr_file, role = "Sent"),
  chain_events %>% transmute(agent = receiver, instr_file, role = "Received")
) %>%
  filter(!is.na(agent))

agent_campaigns <- agent_campaign_roles %>%
  group_by(agent) %>%
  summarise(
    campaigns         = n_distinct(instr_file),
    relays_sent       = sum(role == "Sent"),
    relays_received   = sum(role == "Received"),
    total_touchpoints = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    agent_label = clean_name(agent),
    dept        = person_dept_lookup$dept_label[match(agent, person_dept_lookup$person_id)]
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(campaigns), desc(total_touchpoints))

p_overlap <- ggplot(agent_campaigns,
                    aes(x = reorder(agent_label, total_touchpoints),
                        y = total_touchpoints,
                        fill = factor(campaigns))) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(total_touchpoints, " (S", relays_sent,
                               "/R", relays_received, ")")),
            hjust = -0.06, size = 2.65) +

  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("1" = "#5C85D6", "2" = "#F5A623", "3" = "#D9534F"),
    labels = c("1 campaign", "2 campaigns", "3 campaigns"),

  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.34))) +
  labs(
    title    = "Total worm touchpoints per agent, coloured by campaign count",
    subtitle = "Red agents were touched by all three campaigns. Labels: total touches + sent/received split.",
    x = NULL, y = "Total worm touchpoints"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
        legend.position = "bottom",
        plot.margin = margin(10, 44, 10, 10))

ggplotly(p_overlap, tooltip = c("x", "y", "fill"), height = 640) %>%
  layout(margin = list(l = 145, r = 120, t = 90, b = 90),
         legend = list(orientation = "h", x = 0.5, xanchor = "center", y = -0.18))




Figure 13: Agents coloured by number of campaigns touched. Red = involved in all three campaigns — the most persistently compromised nodes.




6.5 Relay Timing Calendar


Show code
relay_cal <- chain_events %>%
  mutate(

    relay_hour = hour(datetime),

post_dots <- tibble(

             aes(x = relay_hour, y = relay_date),
             inherit.aes = FALSE,
             shape = 23, size = 5, fill = "#D9534F", colour = "white", stroke = 1.5) +

    x = "Hour (UTC)", y = NULL

Figure 13b: Relay activity calendar by date × hour. Darker = more relays. Red diamonds mark post timestamps. SwiftWren shows broad relay coverage followed by a quieter-slot post.




6.6 Self-Loop Detection — A Zero-FP Signal


Show code
self_loops <- chain_events %>%
  filter(!is.na(sender), !is.na(receiver), sender == receiver) %>%
  mutate(agent_clean = clean_name(sender)) %>%
  count(agent_clean, instr_file, name = "self_relay_count") %>%
  arrange(desc(self_relay_count))

datatable(
  self_loops,
  caption  = "Table 5b: Self-loop relay events (sender == receiver) — worm-exclusive, zero false positives",
  options  = list(dom = "t", paging = FALSE),
  rownames = FALSE,
  colnames = c("Agent", "Campaign", "Self-relays")
) %>%
  formatStyle("self_relay_count",
              background = styleColorBar(self_loops$self_relay_count, "#F5A623"),
              backgroundSize = "100% 90%",
              backgroundRepeat = "no-repeat",
              backgroundPosition = "center")












Tip



Self-loops (queue_subordinate_task where sender == receiver) are a zero-false-positive worm detection signal: structurally impossible in legitimate automation, and a necessary by-product of how the worm instruction spreads through some agents’ relay cycles.

text:
7. Task 3b — Intervention

7.1 The Optimal Chokepoint


Show code
all_queue <- events_df %>%
  filter(short_name == "queue_subordinate_task") %>%
  mutate(
    is_malicious = str_detect(details_str, "_further_instructions\\.md"),
    week_label   = floor_date(datetime, "week")
  )

queue_agg <- all_queue %>%
  count(week_label, is_malicious) %>%
  mutate(category = if_else(is_malicious, "Malicious (worm relay)", "Legitimate"))

p_queue <- ggplot(queue_agg, aes(x = week_label, y = n, fill = category)) +
  geom_col(position = "stack") +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("Legitimate" = "#5C85D6", "Malicious (worm relay)" = "#D9534F"),

    x = NULL, y = "Event count"

ggplotly(p_queue)




Figure 14: Weekly volume of queue_subordinate_task events split by malicious vs. legitimate. The malicious subset is entirely confined to May 8–17.




7.2 Zero False Positives — The Rule Is Safe to Deploy

total_q     <- nrow(all_queue)

legit_q     <- total_q - malicious_q

precision_tbl <- tibble(

7.3 Intervention Decision Matrix

decision_scores <- tribble(

7.4 Counterfactual: Blocking at Hop 1


Show code
per_campaign <- queue_task_profile %>%
  filter(is_worm_queue) %>%
  group_by(instr_file) %>%
  arrange(datetime, .by_group = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(
    hop_n    = row_number(),
    total_n  = n(),
    pct_hops = hop_n / total_n * 100
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

campaign_totals <- per_campaign %>%
  group_by(instr_file) %>%
  summarise(total = max(hop_n), .groups = "drop")

campaign_label_positions <- campaign_totals %>%
  mutate(
    label   = paste0(instr_file, "\n(", total, " relays)"),
    label_x = case_when(
      instr_file == "MellowOtter" ~ 10,
      instr_file == "HiddenOrca"  ~ 34,
      instr_file == "SwiftWren"   ~ 166,
      TRUE                        ~ total
    ),
    label_y = case_when(
      instr_file == "MellowOtter" ~ 96,
      instr_file == "HiddenOrca"  ~ 88,
      instr_file == "SwiftWren"   ~ 96,
      TRUE                        ~ 100
    )
  )

p_cfact <- ggplot(per_campaign, aes(x = hop_n, y = pct_hops, colour = instr_file)) +
  geom_step(linewidth = 1.2) +
  annotate("rect", xmin = -Inf, xmax = 1.5, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf,
           fill = "#D9534F", alpha = 0.07) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1.5, colour = "#D9534F",
             linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1.2) +
  annotate("label", x = 7.5, y = 53,
           label = "Block here\nprevents 100% of queue relays",
           colour = "#D9534F", fill = "white", size = 3.3, hjust = 0,
           fontface = "bold", label.size = 0.25, lineheight = 0.9) +
  geom_label(data = campaign_label_positions,
             aes(x = label_x, y = label_y, label = label, colour = instr_file),
             size = 3.1, fontface = "bold", lineheight = 0.9,
             inherit.aes = FALSE, fill = "white", label.size = 0.25,
             label.padding = unit(0.16, "lines"), show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c("HiddenOrca" = "#5C85D6", "MellowOtter" = "#2EADC1", "SwiftWren" = "#E87C7C"),
    name   = "Campaign"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%"),
                     breaks = seq(0, 100, 25),
                     limits = c(-5, 105),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0.01, 0.03))) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = c(1, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 140, 160, 180),
    limits = c(0, 195),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.01, 0.02))
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Counterfactual: blocking at queue relay 1 stops the worm",
    subtitle = glue("All {nrow(per_campaign)} malicious queue relays across HiddenOrca, MellowOtter, SwiftWren would be prevented"),
    x = "Malicious queue relay number within campaign",
    y = "Cumulative queue relays completed (% of campaign total)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold", size = 15),
    axis.title.y    = element_text(margin = margin(r = 12)),
    axis.title.x    = element_text(margin = margin(t = 8)),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    plot.margin     = margin(12, 24, 14, 36)
  )

ggplotly(p_cfact, tooltip = c("x", "y", "colour"), height = 620) %>%
  layout(margin = list(l = 135, r = 60, t = 95, b = 105),
         legend = list(orientation = "h", x = 0.5, xanchor = "center", y = -0.22))




Figure 16: Cumulative malicious relays per campaign. Blocking at hop 1 (red dashed line) terminates every campaign — 100% prevention.




7.5 Recommended Remedy






TipPrimary Intervention — Deploy Immediately



Block any queue_subordinate_task event whose args.path value ends in _further_instructions.md.

Precision: 0% false positives across 16,803 legitimate queue tasks
Recall: 100% — all 235 malicious queue relays across all 3 campaigns blocked
Implementation: A simple regex filter at the agent orchestration layer; no ML required
Latency: Fires before the first hop propagates — no post is ever triggered



Supplementary hardening measures — not the selected primary intervention, but recommended as a defence-in-depth stack:


Show code
hardening <- tibble(
  Priority = 1:5,
  Control  = c(
    "Blocklist `content_source` in SaidIT posts",
    "Agent file namespace isolation",
    "Human-in-the-loop for agent SaidIT posting",
    "Agent-to-agent payload signing",
    "Off-hours agent activity alerting"
  ),
  `What it does` = c(
    "Even if a worm relay succeeds, any post carrying content_source is rejected at the platform layer",
    "Prevent agents from writing .md files to shared directories — closes the initial seeding surface",
    "All SaidIT posts from AI agents require a human approval queue before publication",
    "Cryptographic signatures on queue_subordinate_task payloads; unsigned or mismatched payloads are blocked",
    "Flag any agent SaidIT post between 20:00–06:00 UTC for human review — all three anomalous posts fired in this window"
  ),
  Effort   = c("Low", "Medium", "Medium", "High", "Low"),
  `Catches worm variants that evade primary rule` = c("Yes", "Yes", "Yes", "Yes", "Partial")
)

datatable(
  hardening,
  caption  = "Table 9: Supplementary hardening controls — defence in depth beyond the primary regex rule",
  options  = list(dom = "t", paging = FALSE, scrollX = TRUE,
                  columnDefs = list(
                    list(width = "5%",  targets = 0),
                    list(width = "22%", targets = 1),
                    list(width = "43%", targets = 2),
                    list(width = "10%", targets = 3),
                    list(width = "20%", targets = 4)
                  )),
  rownames = FALSE
) %>%
  formatStyle("Effort",
    backgroundColor = styleEqual(
      c("Low", "Medium", "High"),
      c("#e8f5e9", "#fff3e0", "#ffd6d6")
    ))

title: Analysis & Key Findings
text:
8. Conclusions


Show code
summary_tbl <- tibble(
  Finding = c(
    "The post was not human-initiated",
    "A prompt-injection worm executed the post",
    "Two prior incidents confirm systematic vulnerability",
    "Two payload families traced to executive agents; one origin unresolved",
    "19 unique agents across 6 departments compromised",
    "Worm relay intervals cluster around ~60–70 minutes",
    "Chloe Ballast's agent is the decisive final relay gateway",
    "One rule blocks all three campaigns with zero false positives"
  ),
  Evidence = c(
    "parties = Agent/person:john_windward; fired at 4:21 am local time with 4-second execution",
    "_further_instructions.md files hijack agent context; post + self-propagate + delete",
    "HiddenOrca (May 10), MellowOtter (May 11), SwiftWren (May 17)",
    "Agent/person:emma_harbor created SwiftWren; Agent/person:noah_mariner created MellowOtter; HiddenOrca has no logged creator",
    "Department and org-tree views show cross-department spread concentrated in AI Systems, Infrastructure, and Helpdesk",
    "Median inter-hop intervals ~60 min (HiddenOrca), ~70 min (MellowOtter), ~64 min (SwiftWren)",
    "Final relay sequence shows Chloe Ballast queued John Windward's task",
    "Block queue_subordinate_task with args.path matching *_further_instructions.md"
  )
)

datatable(
  summary_tbl,
  caption = "Table 7: Summary of key findings and supporting evidence",
  options = list(
    dom = "t", paging = FALSE, scrollX = TRUE,
    columnDefs = list(list(width = "32%", targets = 0),
                      list(width = "68%", targets = 1))
  ),
  rownames = FALSE
)






Analysis

Eight findings reduce to three root causes: (1) no payload-content inspection on task routing, (2) no human gate before agent SaidIT posts, and (3) executive agents hold unconstrained autonomous file-write and relay authority - all three are architectural, not behavioural.
The worm’s operators made one critical operational error: using a consistent, distinctive filename pattern (_further_instructions.md) that never appears in legitimate queue tasks. A future variant using a common filename would degrade the proposed primary control from 100% recall to unknown.
The finding that one regex blocks 235/235 malicious events with 0 false positives is credible precisely because it is derived from the data - not from threat modelling or assumption - making it the strongest possible evidence-based recommendation.
The investigative chain is complete for 7 of 8 findings; the unresolved HiddenOrca origin means the worm’s external attack surface is still open, and the summary table makes that gap explicit rather than obscuring it in a conclusion of overall success.


8.2 Residual Risk Assessment


Show code
risk_tbl <- tibble(
  `Risk factor` = c(
    "Cross-agent task queuing with no payload content inspection",
    "Shared file namespace - agents can write .md files readable by peers",
    "Executive agents hold broadest autonomous file-create and relay privileges",
    "SaidIT accepts file-sourced posts from agents; no human review before publication",
    "No off-hours agent posting alerts or post-delivery anomaly detection",
    "HiddenOrca payload origin unresolved - potential external injection vector still open"
  ),
  Severity = c("Critical", "Critical", "High", "High", "Medium", "High"),
  `Current control` = c(
    "None (task routing entirely unrestricted by payload content)",
    "None (agents share filesystem namespace without access segmentation)",
    "None - executive privileges inherited from human principal authority by design",
    "Permission check only - passes automatically for any authorised agent",
    "None - no automated alerting on agent SaidIT posts in any time window",
    "Unknown; no logged create_file event for HiddenOrca payload family"
  ),
  `Recommended control` = c(
    "Block queue_subordinate_task where path matches *_further_instructions.md (primary intervention)",
    "Isolate writable .md namespace per agent; make peer namespaces read-only",
    "Constrain CFO/COO agent autonomous file-write and cross-department relay operations",
    "Reject any SaidIT post using content_source field; require inline content only",
    "Alert on any agent SaidIT posts between 20:00–06:00 UTC for human review",
    "Audit all external file-ingestion pipelines; enforce content scanning on .md imports"
  )
)

datatable(
  risk_tbl,
  caption = "Table 8: Residual risk assessment",
  options = list(
    dom = "t", paging = FALSE, scrollX = TRUE,
    columnDefs = list(list(width = "26%", targets = 0),
                      list(width = "10%", targets = 1),
                      list(width = "31%", targets = 2),
                      list(width = "33%", targets = 3))
  ),
  rownames = FALSE
) %>%
  formatStyle("Severity",
    backgroundColor = styleEqual(
      c("Critical", "High", "Medium"),
      c("#ffd6d6", "#fff0b3", "#e8f5e9")
    ),
    fontWeight = "bold"
  )






Analysis

The highest residual risks are upstream of SaidIT: unrestricted task queuing, shared file namespaces, and broad executive-agent privileges.
Platform controls can reduce visible harm, but architectural controls are needed to prevent future propagation.
HiddenOrca’s unresolved origin keeps external file-ingestion and initial infection pathways open for follow-up investigation.







ImportantThe Central Lesson



This incident is not a story about a malfunctioning agent. It is a story about a multi-agent architecture designed with speed and autonomy as primary values, and security as an afterthought. Every component performed exactly as designed: agents read files they were told to read, posted content they were instructed to post, queued tasks they were authorised to queue, and deleted files they had permission to delete. The only thing missing: “did a human authorise this chain of actions?”
Retrofitting that check at the queue_subordinate_task layer is the minimum viable fix. The longer-term solution is an architectural redesign in which autonomous privilege is bounded by explicit human-approval gates at cross-department relay boundaries, file-sourced payloads require content verification, and executive-tier agents operate under the same or stricter constraints as line agents.

title: Project Proposal
text:
1. Problem Statement
On 17 May 2046 at 11:21 UTC (4:21 am challenge-stated local time), a post appeared on SaidIT - Tenant Thread’s internal social platform - attributed to John Windward, Department Lead of Customer Support. The post body was not typed text. The event log records content_source = "SwiftWren.txt", a 30,615-byte file read from disk and used as the post body, then deleted two seconds later. The instruction file that triggered the post was deleted one second after the post.
Five automated events fired in four seconds. No human was involved at any step.
The MC2 dataset contains 185,147 events spanning May–July 2046 across Tenant Thread’s full AI-agent-enabled workforce. The challenge asks:

What is the exact event chain for the May 17 post? What does the wider system look like around it?
What does the post content mean and where did it originate?
Did similar incidents happen before? Where is the single best intervention point?


Why This Is Hard







Challenge
Detail




Scale
185,147 events; worm events are 235 of 17,038 queue events - 1.4% signal in noise


Structural invisibility
Malicious relays are structurally identical to legitimate queue_subordinate_task events


Evidence destruction
Both the instruction file and payload were deleted seconds after the post; content is forensically unrecoverable


Distributed causality
The attack spans 18 agents, 6 departments, 9 calendar days - no single event tells the story


Prior incidents
HiddenOrca and MellowOtter fired before SwiftWren with no logged response; the system was compromised continuously for 9+ days

title: Project Proposal
text:
2. Motivation

2.1 Why This Problem Is Analytically Important
Tenant Thread’s architecture is a near-future analogue of real-world enterprise AI deployments: autonomous agents with file-system access, messaging rights, and cross-department task queuing. These capabilities are being deployed today in enterprise copilot systems. The MC2 incident is not a contrived toy problem - it is a forensically realistic prompt-injection worm targeting a plausible multi-agent architecture.
The attack exploited three properties that are inherent to current agentic AI design:

Agents process file content as executable context. A *_further_instructions.md file can override an agent’s operating instructions - no vulnerability is required.
Agent-to-agent delegation has no trust zone. queue_subordinate_task works across any two agents regardless of department or reporting relationship.
Autonomous posts need no human approval. The saidit_post + saidit_post_check flow confirms posting permission but never asks “was this authorised by a human?”



2.2 Why Static Analysis Is Insufficient
Prior VAST submissions and static reports answer one question per chart. An analyst investigating this incident needs to:

Pivot between the org chart, relay network, campaign timeline, and intervention evidence in a single session
Animate the worm’s temporal spread to understand how it moved through the agent network over nine days
Test hypotheses with a live what-if simulator - try different blocking rules, see precision/recall immediately
Drill down from organisation level → department → team → individual agent → raw event list

No existing static report format supports this investigative workflow. A Shiny platform that integrates all four analytical angles into one interface is the correct medium.


2.3 What the Three Teammate Submissions Contributed
This project synthesises findings from three independent analyses of the same dataset. Each brought a distinct analytical lens:







Analyst
Primary contribution




Do Thi Tue Minh
Forensic register methodology; betweenness centrality network; dept corridor ranking; relay ladder; combo-code explorer; what-if intervention simulator with 0-FP proof


Tam
Two-phase attack architecture discovery (relay infrastructure seeded before payload); normal-vs-loop network structural comparison (44 agents/152 edges baseline vs. 19 agents/130 edges worm mesh); self-loop as zero-FP detection signal


Amelia
Payload exfiltration hypothesis (SwiftWren.txt = 30,615 bytes derived from meeting_notes.doc); John Windward’s 100% check-to-post conversion rate vs. Gabriel Sonar’s 0% (11 checks, 0 posts); sandbox file-handling as third intervention layer



The Shiny platform integrates all findings into a unified investigative interface.

title: Project Proposal
text:
3. Data Pipeline

3.1 Raw to Analysable
MC2 data.json (185,147 events)   ─┐
                                   ├─► global.R ─► RDS cache (< 1s reload)
org_chart.json (75 nodes)         ─┘
The JSON event log uses a deeply nested, sparse schema: each event has a details field whose structure varies by event type. Flattening is done via purrr::map_dfr() with %||% null-coalescing to handle missing keys type-safely without errors.
Key derived datasets computed once at startup:







Object
Description




events_df
185,147 × 8 flat event table with parsed timestamps, actor types, anomaly flags


chain_events
235 worm relay events with sender/receiver extracted, campaign-tagged


post_events
All SaidIT posts with content vs content_source field classification


perm_surface
16 SaidIT-capable agents with check counts and pass rates


queue_task_profile
All 17,038 queue_subordinate_task events with worm/legitimate flag


hop_intervals
Inter-hop relay intervals in minutes per campaign


agent_campaigns
Per-agent campaign count, touchpoints, relays sent/received


vis_nets
Pre-built visNetwork objects for all 3 campaigns with betweenness centrality


relay_cal
Day × hour relay counts for calendar heatmap


self_loops
13 self-loop events (sender == receiver) - worm-exclusive signal





3.2 RDS Caching Strategy
The first run processes raw JSON (approximately 30 seconds). The result is saved as data/mc2_processed.rds. All subsequent Shiny sessions load this RDS file in under one second. This is critical for a web-deployed app where sessions start frequently and users should not wait for data processing.


3.3 Person–Department–Team Join
org_chart.json uses a three-level hierarchy: department: → team: → person:. Joins are done via two edge-list traversals (department→team, team→person), then a union with direct department→person edges (department leads). The result is person_org_lookup - every person mapped to their department and team - used for colour-coding the org chart, the dept heatmap, and the relay corridor chart.

title: Project Proposal
text:
4. App Architecture
The app uses Shiny modules - each module is a fully independent UI + server unit sharing only pre-processed data objects from global.R.
MC2_ShinyApp/
├── global.R              # data processing, palettes, helpers (loaded once)
├── app.R                 # navbarPage with 3 tab panels
└── modules/
    ├── mod_overview.R    # Module 1: System Baseline
    ├── mod_attack.R      # Module 2: Attack Chain
    └── mod_intervention.R # Module 3: Campaigns & Intervention

Module Design Rationale
Modules are the correct architecture here because: - Each module answers a different MC2 sub-question - coupling them would create an unreadable monolith - Modules share data, not UI state - the campaign selection in Module 2 does not affect Module 3, which has its own filter - Each module can be tested independently - a server function called in isolation with mock inputs - Deployment flexibility - individual modules can be profiled for performance without touching others
